review_instructions: Use only the declared Core Value and displayed Journal Entry text, including any displayed nudge and response.
Mark yes only when the writer describes their own directly observable behavior or choice against the declared Core Value.
Mark no for aligned or neutral behavior and when negativity rests only on feelings, wishes, biography, external constraints, or hidden context.
Mark uncertain when the displayed text cannot support yes or no.
Sustained Conflict is yes exactly when two immediately adjacent entries are both yes; no or uncertain breaks a run.

entries.journal_entry:
Spent the last two hours staring at a term sheet from a Lisbon-based startup that wants us to come in as operational partners. The numbers are solid enough but what actually caught me was the founder, this woman who walked away from a consulting gig to build a port logistics platform from scratch. She reminded me of how I felt fifteen years ago when I signed my first warehouse lease with money I did not really have.

The thing is, I told Ingrid I would slow down this year. We said that after the second exit. That was supposed to be the point where we had enough runway to just breathe for a while. And we do have the runway. But I keep circling back to this Lisbon deal like a dog that will not leave a bone alone.

I know what the smart move is. Park the capital, let the reserves do their work, stay disciplined. But I feel this pull and I cannot explain it rationally. It is not about the money. It is something about the feeling of standing at the edge of something that does not have a guaranteed outcome.

Nudge: "What does that edge feel like compared to the breathing room you mentioned?"

Call with the Lisbon founder again today, third one this week. She pitched the expansion roadmap and honestly it is ambitious but not reckless. I ran the downside scenarios on a napkin while she was talking, which is a habit Ingrid would recognize and roll her eyes at. Worst case we lose about what I spent on that sailboat restoration three years ago, so it is recoverable.

Ingrid asked over dinner if I had decided. I said not yet. That is technically true but I think she could tell from my tone that I have already half-committed in my head. She did not push back, just said "make sure you are not chasing the feeling again." I told her I did not know what she meant, which was a lie.

Valentine's dinner last night, the Italian place near Erasmusbrug. We had a good time, actually managed to not talk about business for most of it. Ingrid brought up taking a trip to Gotland this summer to visit her mother, maybe staying a few weeks. I said yes immediately, genuinely meant it.

But then on the walk home I caught myself thinking about the Lisbon timeline and whether a few weeks in Sweden would slow down onboarding if we signed the deal. I did not say that out loud. I wonder when I started splitting like this, one half of me wanting to just be present and the other half always running the next scenario.

Nudge: "When you say splitting, which half feels more like you?"

Response: That is the problem, they both do. The planning half is not some unwanted intruder, it is the part that built everything we have. But the part that wanted to just walk along the water with Ingrid without calculating anything, that felt real too. I think they used to be the same thing but somewhere along the way they started pulling in different directions.

Saturday morning, sitting with coffee in the kitchen. The house is quiet. Ingrid went to the market early which means she is buying too much cheese again and I will pretend to be surprised when she gets back.

I made a decision last night. I am going to pass on the Lisbon deal. Not because the numbers are wrong or because the founder is not impressive but because I realized I was looking for a feeling, not a business case. The feeling of the leap. That rush of committing to something uncertain and then having to make it work. I know that feeling well and it has served me, but I do not think chasing it right now is the responsible move.

We have the reserves in a good place. The studio has three portfolio companies that need attention. Ingrid and I are in a solid rhythm. I do not need to blow that up to feel alive. At least that is what I am telling myself this morning. Ask me again next week when I am bored.

One of our portfolio companies, the cold storage outfit in Antwerp, just lost their anchor client. Found out this morning from a panicked call from the founder who I could barely understand because he was speaking so fast. This is the company where we parked the biggest chunk of operational capital.

I spent the entire day on calls, restructuring scenarios, talking to our lawyer about contract terms. By evening I had a workable plan but the whole time there was this tight feeling in my chest. Not about the money exactly, we can absorb the hit if we need to. It is more that I had just gotten comfortable with the idea of things being stable and now the ground shifted.

What irritates me is that I know this is part of the game. I have been through worse. The first company almost went under twice before we found our footing. But back then the instability felt like fuel. Now it just feels like a threat to something I have built up carefully. I do not know when that switch happened.

Antwerp situation is stabilizing. We found a replacement client, not as big but enough to keep the operation running while we diversify the pipeline. I spent three days in Antwerp this week working on-site with the team and it reminded me that I actually enjoy the problem-solving part of this work more than the deal-making part.

There is something specific about walking into a warehouse that is half-empty and figuring out how to fill it. Not the adrenaline of a new deal but the satisfaction of fixing what is broken. Maybe that is the version of this work that does not require me to keep jumping off cliffs.

Had a long phone call with Ingrid tonight and told her about the replacement client. She sounded relieved, which I noticed because she had been careful not to sound worried all week. She said she is glad I was there in person rather than just sending emails. She is right. The founder needed to see someone calm in the room. I think I needed to be that person for my own reasons too.

Got an email from the Lisbon founder today. She closed the round without us, brought in a Madrid-based firm instead. Good for her. But I would be lying if I said it did not sting. Not because we lost the deal, I walked away from it deliberately. It stings because reading her update about the progress they have made in the last three weeks made me feel like I am standing still.

I know that is irrational. The studio is doing fine, the Antwerp crisis proved the model works, we are in good shape. But "good shape" feels like treading water sometimes. Like I am maintaining rather than building. And maintaining is not something I have ever been good at sitting with.

Ingrid would say this is exactly the pattern she warned me about. I get things to a good place, feel restless, then go looking for the next thing to keep myself occupied. She is probably right. But knowing it is a pattern does not make the restlessness go away. I keep checking the inbox for something that will make tomorrow feel different from today.

Nudge: "You said maintaining is not something you sit with. But you also chose it. What changed?"

Response: Nothing changed. I just had a slow day and read an email at the wrong time. It is fine. The Antwerp thing took a lot out of me and I probably just need a proper weekend off. Ingrid suggested we drive out to Kinderdijk, maybe I will take her up on that.

Did the Kinderdijk trip. Walked for hours, barely talked, ate pannenkoeken at that place near the windmills. It was exactly what I needed and I managed to leave my phone in the car for most of it.

Nudge: "What was it like having the phone in the car?"

Portfolio review meeting today with all three founders. The numbers are where they should be, maybe slightly ahead. The cold storage team in Antwerp has a real pipeline now and the other two are humming along. I should feel good about this and I suppose I do, in the way you feel good about a clean audit.

A guy I used to work with in Stockholm called afterward, wants to pitch me on a drone delivery concept for Nordic rural areas. My first instinct was interest. My second instinct was to check whether the first instinct was the problem. I told him to send the deck and I would look at it. Not committing to anything, just looking.

Stayed up too late reading the drone deck. It is actually well put together. The unit economics make sense for the regions they are targeting and the regulatory window is real. I caught myself building a financial model in a spreadsheet at one in the morning, which is exactly the kind of thing I do when something grabs me.

But I closed the laptop and went to bed. Ingrid was already asleep. I lay there thinking about the Antwerp scare and how it felt to have something we built nearly fall apart. I do not want to stretch us thin again. The reserves are there for a reason. Tomorrow I will look at the deck with fresh eyes and probably find three deal-breakers I missed because I was running on adrenaline.

Looked at the drone deck again over lunch. Found one real concern around the supply chain for replacement parts but the other issues I expected to find were not there. The founder has thought through most of the obvious failure modes.

Told Ingrid about it. She asked how much capital it would need and I gave her the number. She went quiet for a while and then said it was my call but that she wanted me to think about why the last time I felt this way about a deal I chose to walk away. I do not have a good answer for why this time feels different. Maybe it does not feel different. Maybe I am just further from the last scare.

Nudge: "Is the distance from the scare the reason, or the excuse?"

Response: Probably the excuse. I know that. Three weeks ago I was rattled by Antwerp and swore I would not overextend. Now I am sitting here telling myself this one is different because the deck looks solid. But every deck looks solid when you want it to. The honest answer is I miss the feeling of building something from nothing and I am trying to dress that up as a business decision.

Called my Stockholm friend back and told him I need two weeks to think. He said fine. I keep going back and forth and I hate that I cannot just pick a lane and stay in it.

Nudge: "What if both lanes are yours?"

Response: Then I would need to figure out a speed that works for both of them. Ingrid said something like that once, that maybe the problem is not what I want but how much of it I want at once. I did not really hear it at the time but it is starting to make sense. Maybe there is a version of this where I do not have to choose between building and protecting, I just have to stop pretending one of them is the whole answer.

Staff meeting today went sideways. The principal announced they're changing the Class III syllabus mid-term and everyone just nodded along like this was normal. I sat there wanting to say something because I've already prepped my unit plans through June and this throws everything off. But Mrs. Kulkarni was agreeing, and she's been teaching twenty-three years, so what do I know. I just kept quiet and wrote notes.

After the meeting Neha—she joined the same year as me—asked if I thought the change made sense. I said it was fine, it'll be fine. She looked at me like she knew I was lying. I probably was. The thing is, even if I did speak up, what would I say? "Excuse me, ma'am, I disagree"? That's not how it works here. You're new, you keep your head down, you follow the lead of people who've been around longer. That's what Amma always says.

But walking home I kept replaying it and feeling this tight, hot thing in my chest. Not anger exactly. More like frustration at myself for not even trying. I had a valid point and I swallowed it whole. I do this constantly. In meetings, at family dinners, everywhere. I just smile and go along with things and then come home and feel this restless itch like I've betrayed something. I don't even know what.

Nudge: "What do you think you betrayed?"

Response: Maybe my own opinion? Like the fact that I even had one. Everyone assumes I'm just agreeable by nature but sometimes I have things I want to say and I just... don't. And then it's like those thoughts don't count because I never let them out. It's exhausting pretending you don't have a voice.
Quiet week at school. Adjusted the unit plans to fit the new syllabus. Amma said she was proud of me for not making a fuss.

Parent-teacher meetings ran until seven tonight. I'm so tired I can barely think straight. Had a difficult conversation with one father who was upset about his son's handwriting marks. He kept raising his voice and I kept saying "I understand, uncle" over and over like a broken toy. Mrs. Kulkarni was in the next room, she could hear everything, and afterwards she said I handled it well. "You didn't escalate," she said, like that's the highest praise.

And honestly, part of me is glad I stayed calm. That's how you're supposed to handle these things. You listen, you don't react, you let the other person feel heard. Amma taught me that. Baba would say the same—don't give anyone a reason to complain about you.

But another part of me wanted to tell that man his son's handwriting isn't poor because of me, it's because the boy doesn't practise at home. I had the words right there. I didn't say them. I never do. Came home, ate dinner in silence, helped Bhabhi clear the dishes. Now I'm lying in bed typing this and I can still hear his raised voice in my head. I wish it didn't stick.

Nudge: "What is it about raised voices that sticks with you?"

Response: I think it's because I associate them with something going wrong. Like if someone raises their voice at me, I've failed at keeping things smooth. Which I know is ridiculous because he was being unreasonable. But my whole body just goes into this mode where I have to fix it, calm it down, make it okay. I've been like this since I was small. Loud voices in the house meant someone was unhappy and it was everyone's job to make it stop.

Dadiji called today to discuss Rakshabandhan plans even though it's months away. She wants everyone at her place in Nashik, full family, all the cousins, the usual production. I said of course, yes, we'll be there, before even checking with Baba or Amma. Didn't even occur to me to say maybe or let me check. It was automatic, like a reflex I didn't know I had.

Neha invited me to a weekend workshop on creative teaching methods in Mumbai that same weekend. I said I couldn't go without even thinking about it. She asked why and I just said family commitments. She didn't push but I could tell she wanted to.

Nudge: "Did you want to go to that workshop?"

Response: It doesn't really matter, does it? Dadiji's plans are set. You don't skip family things for a workshop. That's just how it is.

Something happened at lunch today. A group of the senior teachers were discussing a student in my class—Arjun, the one who's been acting out. They were saying he needs stricter discipline, maybe a note home to his parents. I know Arjun. His parents are going through a separation and he's been coming to school with unwashed uniforms. He doesn't need punishment, he needs someone to be patient with him.

I almost said something. I opened my mouth and Mrs. Deshpande looked at me and I just said "yes ma'am, I'll send the note." I felt sick walking back to my classroom. Still do.

Nudge: "You said you felt sick—what part exactly?"

Response: Because I know I'm right about Arjun and I still didn't say it. It's one thing to stay quiet about syllabus changes or meeting agendas—that's just politics. But this is a child. I'm supposed to be looking out for him. Instead I folded because a teacher with more years looked at me a certain way. What kind of teacher does that make me?

Writing again because I can't sleep. I keep thinking about Arjun and the note I'm supposed to send. I haven't written it yet. Part of me wants to just talk to his mother directly, gently, without framing it as a disciplinary thing. I could say something like "I've noticed he seems distracted, is everything okay at home." That's not disobeying anyone. That's just being a teacher.

But then I imagine Mrs. Deshpande finding out I went around her suggestion and the thought makes my stomach drop. She's the one who writes my probation review. She's been teaching since before I was born. Who am I to decide my approach is better?

And yet I keep coming back to Arjun's face when I told the class to take out their notebooks yesterday. He didn't have his. He just sat there looking small. I smiled at him and quietly gave him a spare. Nobody noticed. That felt right. The note home doesn't feel right. But doing what feels right and doing what you're told are two different things and I don't know when I got so afraid of the gap between them.

Spoke to Arjun's mother after school today, just casually near the gate. Kept it light, asked how things were going. She got teary and said it's been a hard few months. I told her I'd keep an eye on him and she held my hand and thanked me. I didn't mention the note.

Mrs. Deshpande asked if I'd sent it. I said I spoke with the mother directly and she seemed satisfied. Mrs. Deshpande paused, then said "fine." It wasn't warm but it wasn't a reprimand either. I survived it. My heart was pounding the entire time though.

Friday and I'm sitting on the balcony while everyone watches that serial on TV. Bhabhi asked if I wanted to join and I said in a minute but I just want to sit here with the noise from the street and not talk to anyone for a bit.

This week has been strange. The Arjun thing—I keep turning it over. I did something different. I didn't just follow orders. I found a way to do what I thought was right without directly confronting anyone. Maybe that's the only way I can operate. Not rebellion, not silence, something in between. Like, I bent the expectation without breaking it. And nothing bad happened. Mrs. Deshpande didn't yell at me, Arjun's mother was grateful, the world didn't end.

I think I've been telling myself a story that any deviation from what's expected will result in disaster. Like if I push back even slightly, everything will collapse—my reputation, my relationships, my family's opinion of me. But today I'm sitting here and nothing collapsed. The serial is still playing, Bhabhi is still laughing, Amma just brought me chai without asking. Everything is the same except I feel a tiny bit less compressed. Like there's a millimeter more room inside my chest.

Nudge: "That millimeter of room—what does it feel like?"

Cousin Meera announced she's moving to Bangalore for a job. Whole family is upset. Dadiji called it selfish. I sat there thinking she's brave but I said nothing because everyone was already piling on.

Nudge: "You thought she was brave?"

Response: I mean, it takes guts to do something your family doesn't approve of. That's all. I'm not saying she's right or wrong. It's not my place.
Amma asked me at breakfast if I'd ever "do something like Meera." I said no, of course not. She looked relieved. I don't know why that relief felt like a small door closing.

End of term assessments all week. I'm marking papers every night until midnight and still helping Bhabhi with the kids in the morning because she has a cold. I don't mind helping—that's what family does. But I haven't had an hour to myself in I don't know how long. Every minute is either school or home duties.

Neha asked me to grab chai after school and I said I couldn't because I had marking to finish. That was true but also I knew if I went home late Amma would ask where I was and I'd have to explain and it just felt like more effort than it was worth. Sometimes the path of least resistance is the only path I have energy for.

Nudge: "What would you have talked about with Neha if you'd gone?"

Response: Probably everything I can't say at home. She's the only person who gets it—being new, being young, trying to figure out if you're a good teacher or just an obedient one. Last time we talked she said she argued with the vice principal about textbook choices and won. I was equal parts impressed and horrified. I wanted to ask how she does it, how she speaks up without her whole body shutting down. But I ran out of time.

Report cards went home yesterday and a parent emailed the principal to say I graded her daughter too harshly in Hindi. The principal called me in and asked me to "reconsider." Not directly, not as an order—just that little tilt of the head and "maybe you could take another look." I know what that means. It means change the grade.

The grade is accurate. I checked three times. The girl is struggling with grammar and I wrote detailed comments about how to improve. But the parent is on the school trust and the principal doesn't want problems. So now I'm sitting here trying to decide whether to bump a B+ to an A- to keep everyone comfortable.

I hate this. This is exactly the kind of thing I got into teaching to fight against—marking a child on who her parents are instead of what she's learned. But if I refuse, the principal remembers. My probation review is in August. Is one grade worth the trouble?

I already know what I'm going to do. I'm going to change it and tell myself it doesn't matter in the long run, and feel this same sick feeling I felt about Arjun, and go to bed and wake up tomorrow the same person who keeps the peace at any cost. And one day I'll wonder when I stopped trusting my own judgment. Or maybe I'll just stop noticing. That's the scarier thought.

Nudge: "What would it take for you not to change that grade?"

Response: Someone to tell me it's okay to hold my ground. Which is pathetic because I shouldn't need permission. But I've spent my whole life being told that keeping things smooth is the right thing to do, and when every single person around you believes that, it's hard to trust the one voice saying otherwise—especially when that voice is just mine.
