Orientation session today. Sat in the back row out of habit, then realized I was the only one over forty in the entire lecture hall. The program coordinator kept talking about "building your professional foundation" and I wanted to raise my hand and say that some of us already had a foundation, thank you, before it was demolished without warning.

I kept my mouth shut. That is what you do. You do not embarrass yourself by complaining about circumstances nobody asked about. But walking back to my apartment afterward, I felt it — that heaviness in my chest. I am not sure if this was the right decision. Forty thousand dollars a year to be lectured about organizational behavior by someone who has never actually run an organization.

Nudge: "What would make this decision feel worth it by the time you graduate?"

Response: A title that commands respect again. A role where people come to me for guidance, not the other way around. I suppose I want to walk into a room and have people assume I belong there, the way they used to.

Group project assigned today. I was placed with three twenty-somethings who immediately started a WhatsApp thread I had to ask them to explain how to join. They scheduled our first meeting for 10 PM on a weeknight.

Could not sleep after writing that last note. The 10 PM meeting is bothering me more than it should. Back when I managed the retail lending division, if someone scheduled a meeting for 10 PM, there had better have been an emergency — a systems failure, a regulatory audit, something serious. These kids treat it like nothing.

I know I am supposed to adapt. That is the whole point of coming back to school — proving I can learn new ways of working. But there is a difference between adapting and abandoning every standard you spent a career developing. I ran teams. I built processes. I had an executive assistant who managed my calendar. Now I am asking a twenty-three-year-old named "Brayden" how to download an app on my phone.

The worst part is that I smiled and thanked him. What else was I supposed to do? Throw a tantrum about how things used to be? I have seen those people. They become cautionary tales whispered about in break rooms. So I will show up to the 10 PM meeting and I will contribute my ideas and I will be pleasant about all of it. That is the only path forward.

But I am writing this at 11:47 PM because I cannot stop replaying the moment when Brayden said "it's super easy, here let me show you" with that patient voice you use for elderly relatives who cannot work the remote control.

Nudge: "You mentioned you smiled and thanked him. What would have happened if you'd responded differently — said something honest about how that moment felt?"

Professor Chen called on me in class today and I gave a detailed answer drawing on my experience restructuring our branch network in 2019. She nodded politely and said "that's one perspective, but let's look at what the research shows." Then proceeded to present a case study that contradicted half of what I said.

I stayed after class to discuss it further and she said my "practitioner insights are valuable" but that academic rigor requires us to "question our assumptions." I am not sure what assumptions she thinks I have. I was there. I saw what worked. The branch network I restructured is still operational — or it was until the whole bank got acquired.

My daughter texted me later asking how school was going. I told her it was fine.

Nudge: "When you told your daughter things were fine, was that to protect her from worrying, or something else?"

Response: She has her own problems to deal with. My job is to handle mine.

The 10 PM meeting went until midnight. Brayden talked for forty-five minutes about "leveraging synergies" without saying anything concrete. I drafted the entire project outline afterward because someone had to. Submitted it to the group chat at 2 AM.

Interesting development. The group used my outline almost word for word. Brayden presented it in class as "our collaborative framework" and the professor praised the "clear structure and practical grounding." Nobody mentioned that I wrote it. I did not correct them.

Here is the thing though. I felt something shift when I saw the professor nodding along. That familiar feeling from the old days — when you put something together that works and people respond to it. The recognition does not need to be public. I know what I contributed. The professor knows the quality improved dramatically between our brainstorm and our deliverable.

Maybe this is how it works now. You do the work, you let other people take the stage, you build influence through competence rather than title. I am not sure I like it. But I am starting to see that there might be a way to operate here that is not completely foreign to who I am.

My ex-wife used to say I cared too much about getting credit. She meant it as criticism. Maybe she had a point, or maybe she did not understand what it is like to have your competence be the only thing you can rely on. Either way, the work got done. That matters.

Nudge: "What made you decide not to correct them about the outline? Was it strategic, or did something else hold you back?"

Response: I suppose I was testing something. Whether I could let go of the credit and still feel like myself. And I did, mostly. But there was also this — if I had spoken up, I would have been the old man demanding acknowledgment. I have seen how quickly people dismiss that. Better to let the work speak and see who notices.

Career services appointment today. The counselor — probably thirty years old at most — asked me what kind of "pivot" I was hoping to make. I explained that I am not pivoting, I am rebuilding. She looked confused.

I walked her through my background and she said the market is "challenging" for candidates my age but that I have "transferable skills." Transferable. As if thirty years of leadership is a foreign currency that needs to be exchanged for something more useful. I thanked her for her time and left with a folder of job search resources I will probably never open.

Midterm grades posted. I received the highest score in the section on my case analysis. Professor Chen left a comment: "Exceptional integration of theory and practice." I printed it out and put it in my desk drawer.

Nudge: "Why did you print it? What will having that piece of paper there do for you?"

Response: Evidence. Proof that I was not imagining things — that I do know what I am doing, even in this environment. On the days when I feel like an artifact, I can open that drawer and see that someone with credentials agrees I have something to offer.

Group presentation today. I suggested we rehearse beforehand but was outvoted. "We'll figure it out live," Brayden said. So we went in unrehearsed and it showed — stumbling over transitions, contradicting each other on key points. Professor Chen gave us a B-minus and commented that our "individual insights were strong but integration was lacking."

I wanted to say something afterward but held back. What would be the point? They would just see me as the cranky old guy who cannot roll with informality. Better to take the grade and move on. But it grates. I built my reputation on preparation. On never walking into a room without knowing exactly what I was going to say and why. Watching twenty years of professional instincts get overridden by committee feels like swallowing glass.

Sunday afternoon. First time in weeks I do not have something due tomorrow. Went to a coffee shop near campus and just sat for a while. Watched the students come and go with their laptops and their confidence and their entire futures stretching out in front of them.

I remember that feeling. Not the specifics — I was not a coffee shop person at twenty-five — but the sense that time was on my side. That I could make mistakes and recover. That every door led to another door. Now I feel like I am running out of doors. Or maybe I already picked one and the rest all closed behind me.

Here is what I have been circling around for weeks: I came back to school because I could not stand being unemployed. Being introduced at parties as "Harold, he's figuring out his next chapter." The pity. The sudden availability of career advice from people who had never managed anyone. An MBA gave me something to say when people asked what I was doing. A credential that signals I am still in the game.

But sitting here today, I wonder if that is enough of a reason. If I am just collecting another piece of paper to avoid facing the question of what I actually want from whatever time is left.

Email from a former colleague today. She is now VP at a regional credit union and asked if I would be interested in consulting on a branch optimization project over the summer. The pay is modest but the work is exactly what I know how to do.

I said yes immediately, then spent an hour wondering if I should have negotiated. Old habits. But the truth is I would have done it for free just to be back in a room where people want my opinion, not because of an assignment but because I have answers they need. That probably sounds desperate. Maybe it is.

Nudge: "You said you would have done it for free. What does that tell you about what you're really looking for right now?"

Response: That I need to matter somewhere. The MBA is about credentials, about what looks right on paper. But this — someone calling because they remembered I knew what I was doing — that is about actually being useful. I suppose I have been confusing the two.

End of semester. Submitted my last paper at 11:58 PM. Overall GPA: 3.7. I should feel accomplished. Mostly I feel relieved that I can stop pretending to be something I am not for three months. The consulting project starts Monday.

Had the tech lead conversation with Martijn today. He laid out what the role would look like — running standups, making architecture calls, representing the team in cross-department meetings. I said I was interested, which is true, but the whole time I was thinking about how I'd actually handle it. I build things well when people leave me alone to think. That's not what this role is.

But I also know I did the right thing saying yes. Everyone on the team expects it, Noor would think I was insane to turn it down, and frankly it would look odd to pass on a promotion you're being handed. I can figure out the rest as I go. Probably.

Nudge: "What part of it actually interests you?"

Long week. Spent most of it pairing with the new hire, walking her through the codebase and our deployment pipeline. Didn't get to touch my own backlog at all but she's ramping up faster than the last person we onboarded so I'll take it.

Nudge: "Was it worth not having your own time?"

Sprint retro got a bit heated. Devika suggested we rethink the approval flow for deployments because she thinks it slows us down. She's right, technically — it does add a half-day lag. I had a whole alternative sketched in my head while she was talking, a lighter-weight check that would still catch the critical stuff.

Didn't say it though. The senior architects set up the current flow and they were in the call. Felt like bringing up a redesign right there would come across as me trying to show off, especially with the tech lead thing floating around. So I just nodded and said I could look into Devika's point offline. She seemed a bit deflated that nobody backed her up more directly. I get it.

Weird day. Started normal — coffee, standup, tickets. Then around lunch Pieter from platform team pinged me about a side project he's been hacking on, this internal tool for tracking deployment health across services. He wanted to know if I'd help build the backend. Not sanctioned work, just something he thinks would be useful and wants to pitch to leadership once it's built.

First thought was yes obviously, this is the kind of thing I actually want to spend time on, building something new without twelve layers of sign-off. Second thought was that Martijn would not love me spending cycles on an unsanctioned project right when he's evaluating me for lead. Third thought was that Pieter didn't ask anyone else, he asked me, and that felt good in a way I don't fully understand. Like someone saw the part of me that wants to just build.

I told Pieter I'd think about it over the weekend. Which is code for "I want to do it but I need to figure out if I'm allowed to want to do it." That sounds ridiculous written down. I'm a grown man deciding whether to write some code on the side. But it feels more loaded than that right now.

Told Pieter I'd help with the tool. We set up a repo and I spent an hour last night after Noor went to sleep scaffolding the API layer. Felt more engaged in that hour than I have all sprint on the actual work.

Today was back to normal though — reviewing PRs, answering Slack threads, sitting through a planning session where half the decisions had already been made before we walked in. The usual. I'm not complaining, it's fine. I just noticed the contrast, that's all.

Nudge: "You said it's fine twice now. Is it?"

Response: Yeah. It's work, it doesn't all have to be exciting.

Noor and I went to dinner with her parents in Rotterdam on Saturday. Her dad asked about the promotion and I gave the standard answer — it's in progress, I'm grateful for the opportunity, etc. He seemed pleased. On the train back Noor said she was proud of me and I felt genuinely good about it. Like I'm on a track that makes sense to the people I care about.

Then on Sunday I stayed up way too late working on Pieter's tool and got completely absorbed refactoring the data ingestion layer. Noor asked what I was doing and I said "just work stuff" which wasn't really a lie but wasn't the whole truth either. I don't know why I didn't just explain it. Maybe because explaining it would mean admitting how much more alive that project makes me feel, and then someone might ask what that says about everything else.

Nudge: "What would it mean if you did explain it to her?"

Response: Probably nothing bad. She'd understand. I think the issue is more that saying it out loud makes it real — that the path everyone's excited about for me isn't the one that lights me up. And then I'd have to figure out what to do with that.

Martijn officially put me on the lead shortlist. Two candidates, me and someone from the other squad. I thanked him and said I'd put my best foot forward. Which I will. Whatever doubts I have aren't anyone else's problem to carry.

Team drinks after work. Everyone was congratulating me about the shortlist like it's already decided. I kept saying it wasn't confirmed yet but nobody listens. Devika made a joke about me being her future boss and I laughed but it sat wrong with me for the rest of the evening.

I don't think I'm being fake about it. I genuinely want to do well, and the role comes with better pay, more visibility, things that matter. But there's this gap between how I talk about it to other people and what actually runs through my head at 11pm when I'm staring at Pieter's repo. Not sure what to do with that. For now I'm doing both, which is sustainable enough.

Presented the deployment health tool to Pieter's skip-level manager today. She liked it. Asked if we'd want to bring it under the platform roadmap officially. Said yes without hesitating.

Nudge: "That was quick for someone who usually thinks things over."

Response: Some things don't need overthinking.

Martijn pulled me aside after standup. Apparently someone mentioned to him that I've been spending time on Pieter's tool and he wanted to know if it was affecting my sprint commitments. It isn't — I've been keeping my velocity up — but the way he framed it made it sound like a warning. "Just want to make sure your priorities are clear," he said.

I told him it wouldn't be an issue and that the tool is now being folded into the platform roadmap anyway, so it's legitimate work. He nodded and dropped it. But I've been replaying the conversation all afternoon. The rational part of me knows he was just doing his job. The other part feels like I got caught doing something I wasn't supposed to enjoy. I hate that I felt the need to justify it. I hate more that I did justify it, immediately, without pushing back at all.

Nudge: "What would pushing back have looked like?"

Response: Saying something like "I'm delivering on everything you've asked, so what I do with my remaining time is my call." Which is true. But I keep imagining how that would land — confrontational, difficult, not a team player. So I default to the smooth version. And then I'm annoyed at myself for it later, which is its own kind of exhausting.
Got the tech lead offer. Accepted on the spot because that's what you do. Texted Noor and she sent about fifteen celebration emojis. Calling my parents tonight.

Same day, later. Parents were thrilled, mum teared up a little. Had a glass of wine with Noor and she kept saying she knew it would happen. Everyone's reaction confirmed that this was the right move, or at least the expected one, and those two things feel like they should be different but tonight they're the same and I'm too tired to untangle it.

I keep coming back to something small. When Martijn told me, my first feeling wasn't excitement. It was relief. Relief that it's done, that I don't have to hold the performance anymore, that I can stop wondering and just be in the role. Maybe excitement comes later. Or maybe relief is enough. I genuinely don't know and I'm going to stop thinking about it and go to bed.

Handed in my notice at Zain last week and everyone keeps asking why I'd leave a senior role with stock options. I don't have a clean answer. Something just goes dead inside me when I know exactly what the next six months will look like.

Nudge: "What does that deadness feel like when it starts?"

Response: It's hard to describe. Like the air gets thick. Anyway, the new place starts in two weeks so I'm focused on that now.

First week at NovaPay. The stack is Rust and Go, neither of which I've used professionally before, so I've been staying up past midnight going through documentation. The CTO is 31 and talks faster than anyone I've ever met. The whole office runs on this nervous energy that I find either exhilarating or exhausting depending on the hour.

My wife Dina said I looked happier at dinner last night than I have in months. She's probably right. My mother called from Amman and asked if this is really the time to be starting over, at my age, with the kids' school fees what they are. I told her it's a lateral move, which isn't exactly true — I took a pay cut. But she doesn't need to know that right now.

Second entry today because I just got out of the team standup and I need to note this. They gave me ownership of the payment reconciliation service on day four. At Zain it took me eight months to get write access to the production repo.

Spent the day pair-programming with Farah, one of the junior engineers. She's 26 and already knows Rust better than most people I've worked with. I kept catching myself wanting to impress her with something clever instead of just learning the idioms properly. Old habit. I measure myself against everyone in the room whether I want to or not.

We got the reconciliation pipeline passing all tests by end of day, which felt good. The CTO, Samir, pinged me in Slack to say "nice velocity for week one." I screenshot that. I know it's a small thing but I needed it. At Zain nobody noticed when you shipped. You'd push a feature that handled millions of transactions and the response was silence or a Jira ticket for the next thing.

After work I went to the gym for the first time in weeks. Ran five kilometers on the treadmill and my knees reminded me I'm not thirty anymore. Dina and I took the kids to City Walk for dinner. Layla wanted sushi, Adam wanted burgers, we compromised on a food court. Normal evening. But I was actually present for it instead of mentally rewriting code in my head, which is rare for me during a new project. Maybe that's a good sign.

Got invited to speak at a Go meetup next month about the migration patterns we're using at NovaPay. Said yes immediately. Now I have to actually build something worth presenting about, which is either motivating or terrifying, can't tell which.

Nudge: "What made you say yes before thinking it through?"

Response: Because saying no feels like admitting I'm not the kind of person who does these things. And I want to be that person. The one who ships and talks about it and gets known.

My old colleague Walid messaged me saying NovaPay's last CTO left after six months and the company has a "churn problem." He thinks I made a mistake. I think he's projecting because he's been at the same bank for fifteen years and can't imagine choosing otherwise.

The meetup talk is in three weeks and I haven't started the slides. I keep opening the deck and then getting pulled into something else — a new Kubernetes operator I want to try, a side project idea for a CLI tool that generates API contracts. My brain refuses to stay on one track. The talk would be good for visibility, I know this, and yet every time I sit down to work on it I feel this resistance like I'm doing homework.

Dina asked tonight if I've made any friends at the new company or if it's "all just code again." Fair question. I eat lunch at my desk most days. There's a group that goes to the shawarma place downstairs but I usually skip it because I'm deep in something. I told her I'll try to go next week. She gave me that look that says she's heard this before.

Performance review cycle started this week and Samir asked everyone to write self-assessments. I spent two hours on mine, rewriting it three times. The first draft was too modest, the second was too aggressive, the third I think landed somewhere honest. I listed the reconciliation service, the test coverage improvements, the documentation I rewrote. Five weeks of work and I already have a page of deliverables. That's the thing about joining somewhere new — everything you do is visible.

Had an odd moment during Friday prayers at the mosque. The imam was talking about the difference between ambition that serves your community and ambition that isolates you. I'm not especially religious but it struck a nerve. My father was a civil engineer in Amman who built the same kinds of bridges for thirty years and was beloved by everyone in the neighborhood. I can't imagine staying anywhere for thirty years. Does that make me restless or just different? He seemed content. I don't know what contentment feels like, honestly. The closest I get is the first few months at a new job when everything is a puzzle and I'm learning at full speed.

Dina read part of my self-assessment over my shoulder and said "you really need them to see you, don't you." She wasn't being mean about it. But it sat with me because she's right. I do need that. I've always needed that and I've never really asked myself why.

Gave the Go meetup talk last night. About sixty people showed up. The demo worked — the live migration ran without crashing, which honestly I was sweating about until the last minute. Got solid questions afterward and two people asked for my LinkedIn. Samir was in the audience and mentioned it in the team channel this morning.

Good day. Drove home with the windows down even though it was barely 18 degrees out. Put on Fairuz, which I only listen to when I'm in a specific mood. Dina had kept dinner warm. I told the kids about the talk and Layla said "baba, are you famous now?" which made me laugh more than it should have.

Nudge: "What mattered more — the talk going well or people noticing?"

Three months into NovaPay. The initial rush is starting to flatten. Not gone, but the problems are becoming familiar. I know the codebase now, I know the deployment pipeline, I know where the bodies are buried. That feeling of every day being a discovery is fading into competence, which should be a good thing but it makes me uneasy.

I keep thinking about a conversation with my uncle Faris last Eid. He runs a carpentry shop in Amman, same shop for forty years. I asked him if he ever gets bored and he looked at me like I'd asked something absurd. He said the wood is different every time — different grain, different knots, different mood. Same tools, same hands, but never the same piece. I want to believe that about software but I'm not sure I do.

Had a one-on-one with Samir and he talked about making me tech lead for the new payments vertical. It's a big deal. More responsibility, more visibility, a path to VP if the company scales. On paper it's everything I've been working toward. But there's a part of me that immediately started thinking about what else is out there. A friend forwarded me a job posting for a robotics startup in Abu Dhabi doing computer vision for warehouse automation. Completely different domain. I bookmarked it and I don't know why. I have a good thing here. Why can't I just stay with it?

Nudge: "What would happen if you stayed this time?"

Dina found the Abu Dhabi job posting in my browser history. She didn't yell, just got very quiet, which is worse. I told her I was just looking, which is true. Everyone looks.

Nudge: "What was it like when she went quiet?"

Response: Like she was doing math in her head. Calculating how many times we've done this. The kids changed schools twice in four years because of me. I know she's tired of it. I'm tired of it too, honestly. But the looking feels like breathing — I don't know how to stop.

Second entry today because I can't sleep. It's 1am and I've been lying here thinking about the tech lead offer. Samir wants an answer by Monday. The role is everything that would look right on a CV — leadership, equity, a team of eight reporting to me. My father would have understood this kind of trajectory even if he didn't understand the technology. Build something, get recognized, build more.

But then I picture myself in six months running the same standups, managing the same sprint cycles, dealing with stakeholder calls about roadmap timelines. The operational weight of it. I know the excitement would drain out and I'd be left holding a title that looks impressive but feels like a cage. This has happened before. At Zain, at the logistics company before that, at the consulting firm before that. The pattern is so clear that I could draw it on my whiteboard — six months of fire, six months of friction, then I leave.

I don't know what I actually want. I thought I wanted to prove I could build things that matter and have people notice. And I can. I have. But the moment it becomes routine I start suffocating. And I don't know if the problem is the jobs or if the problem is me. Dina asked me once, years ago, whether I'm running toward something or away from something. I laughed it off. Tonight it doesn't feel funny.

Nudge: "Dina's question — what do you think now?"

Response: I think I'm doing both. Running toward the next thing because it makes me feel alive, and running from the part where being good at something stops being enough and you actually have to sit with yourself. My father could sit with himself. I never learned how. Maybe that's what I'm actually avoiding — not boredom, but stillness.

Told Nadia she could take the summer to think about London but that we would discuss it seriously in June. She looked relieved but I felt nothing. I keep wondering if I am just delaying because I do not know how to say no to her face.

Nudge: "What would saying no actually cost you?"

Response: More than the argument itself. I think it would mean admitting the business is not enough to hold the family together the way it held us when Baba was running it.

My cousin Faisal called to say his son is taking over his construction firm this year. He did not say it to make a point, but it landed like one. His kid is twenty-three and already stepping in. Meanwhile I cannot even get Nadia to visit the warehouse.

People keep acting like I should just let her go do whatever she wants. Rania says I am being stubborn. But nobody asks what happens to this company in fifteen years. Nobody asks who carries it forward. I did not choose this either, but I showed up.

Nudge: "Did you want someone to ask you that back then?"

Response: That was a different time. There was no question to ask.

The new Italian distributor wants us to rebrand. Sleeker labels, English-only packaging, drop the family crest. He said the current look feels "regional." I almost hung up on him. Regional. My father designed that label with a calligrapher from Salt in 1982. It has been on every bottle we have ever shipped.

I brought it up with Samir, my operations manager, and he shrugged and said maybe the Italian has a point. That the European market responds to minimalism. I asked him if he would strip his grandmother's embroidery off the wall because a decorator said it clashed with the furniture. He laughed. I was not joking.

Rania told me later I am letting sentiment run the business. Maybe. But if I strip away everything that connects this company to where it came from, what exactly am I running? A logistics operation with my name on it. That is not what Baba built and it is not what I agreed to carry.

Could not sleep last night. Kept turning over the rebranding conversation. Went down to the kitchen at two in the morning and found Baba's old notebooks in the drawer where Mama keeps the good tablecloths. He used to write down every buyer's name, where they were from, what they ordered. Pages of it in his careful handwriting.

I sat there reading for an hour. He wrote about a French buyer in 1991 who wanted to change the oil blend and he refused. Lost the account. Got it back three years later when the buyer admitted the original was better. I do not know if that story is supposed to comfort me or scare me. The market is different now.

The thing that unsettles me is not the Italian distributor. It is that part of me can see his point. The rational part. And I do not know what it means that I can see it and still feel like acting on it would be a kind of betrayal. Like I would be the one who finally broke the thread.

Nudge: "What does that thread hold together for you?"

Response: Everything. The way we do business, yes, but also who I am in relation to my father. If I change the way he built things, I am saying his way was not good enough. And I am not ready to say that, even if the numbers eventually force me to.

Nadia brought home a prospectus from the London film school. Left it on the kitchen table like it was a magazine. Rania pretended not to notice. I noticed.

She has been building her case quietly. Showing us short films she made on her phone, mentioning alumni who work at Al Jazeera and BBC Arabic. She is smart about it. She knows a direct argument will not work with me so she is laying evidence like a lawyer. I would admire it if it were not aimed at dismantling the one plan I had for this family's future.

My brother Kamal called and said I should let her go, that holding her back will only push her further away. Easy for him to say. He moved to Dubai fifteen years ago and calls on holidays. He has no idea what it takes to keep things together here. To sit at Mama's table every Friday and hold the center while everyone else drifts toward their own orbits. Someone has to be the anchor and apparently that someone is always me.

Nudge: "Is being the anchor something you chose or something that happened to you?"

Response: Both, I think. It happened because I was the eldest and stayed. But I also chose it every single time I could have left. The problem is I chose it so many times it stopped feeling like a choice and started feeling like who I am. I do not know how to separate the two anymore.

Long week. Two shipments delayed because of port congestion in Aqaba, had to personally call four buyers to explain. Spent most of Thursday driving to Irbid because Mama's water heater broke and my brother the Dubai expert could not exactly fix it from there.

Came home to Rania and the kids eating mansaf without me. They saved me a plate. Ate it cold at ten o'clock watching the news. Just one of those weeks where everything takes twice as long and nothing feels finished.

Actually, the mansaf was good even cold. Rania uses Mama's recipe, the one with the dried yogurt from the women's co-op in Ajloun. Small thing but it reminded me that some things do carry forward without anyone forcing them.

Quarterly numbers came in and they are flat. Not terrible, but flat in a market that is growing means we are losing ground. Samir put together a presentation about expanding into the Gulf retail market. New packaging, smaller bottles, gift sets for Ramadan. I told him to go ahead with the proposal.

I did not fight it this time. Maybe because I am tired, maybe because the port delays last week reminded me how fragile our European pipeline really is. Diversifying makes sense. I told myself the Gulf packaging can look different from the European line without touching the original label. A compromise. Baba would have called it something else.

Nadia asked me at dinner if she could shadow a documentary crew in Amman next week. Some university project. I said fine without really thinking about it. Rania gave me a look, like she was surprised I did not put up a fight. I did not have one in me tonight.

Nudge: "Are these concessions or decisions?"

Took the kids to Friday prayers at the old mosque near Jabal Amman instead of our usual one. The imam there knew my father. Afterward he told my youngest, Omar, a story about how Baba once donated an entire shipment to a refugee camp in the eighties. Omar's eyes went wide. He had never heard that one.

Drove home with all three kids in the back seat arguing about whether Jiddo was a hero or just generous. Nadia said both. I did not correct anyone. It was a good morning.

Nudge: "What would you have corrected?"

Response: That he was not being generous. He was being obligated. In our family, in our community, you give because that is what you do. It is not heroism, it is just the way things work. But watching Omar hear it as something extraordinary made me realize maybe that way of doing things is not as obvious to them as it was to us. And that worries me.

Nadia came back from the documentary shoot glowing. She talked through dinner about camera angles and interview techniques. I listened and felt two things at once: pride and something heavier I cannot name.

The Italian distributor sent a revised proposal. They will keep the family crest but want to modernize the typography and add English and Italian text below the Arabic. Samir thinks it is a fair middle ground. I stared at the mockup for twenty minutes.

It looks fine. Objectively, it looks fine. The crest is there, the Arabic is there, just smaller. And I keep telling myself that Baba adapted too, that the company looked different in 1995 than it did in 1978. But he was the one making those changes. He earned the right to decide what stayed and what evolved. I inherited the decision and it does not sit the same way.

Rania asked me tonight if I have thought more about Nadia and London. I told her not now. She said there will never be a right time and she is probably correct. The June deadline I set is almost here and I do not have an answer. I do not even have a framework for the answer. Just this weight in my chest every time I think about the company ten years from now with no family in it.

Nudge: "What if the company survives but looks nothing like what your father built?"

Told Nadia she can apply to London. She cried. I cried. Rania stood in the doorway pretending she was not crying. The house felt lighter after, like we had been holding our breath for weeks.

Nudge: "What changed between last week and now?"

Response: Nothing changed, really. I just stopped waiting for the feeling to go away and made the decision with it still there. Maybe that is what my father did too. Carried the weight and moved anyway.

Today was the monthly temple committee meeting and I just cannot shake this strange feeling. Meena aunty said something about how "some people like to take charge of everything" and I know she was talking about me. Everyone was looking at their phones or the floor. I have been running this committee for six years and we have never had a failed event, never run out of food, never had volunteers not show up. But suddenly I am the problem?

I stayed quiet. What else could I do? If I defended myself it would look exactly like what she was accusing me of. But sitting there pretending I did not hear her felt like swallowing glass. My husband said I should just ignore it, that Meena aunty is just jealous because her daughter-in-law moved her out of the main house. Maybe. But that does not explain why no one else spoke up for me.

The thing is, I take charge because if I do not, things fall apart. Remember the Holi celebration last year when I was at Ravi's parents' house? Complete disaster. Cold food, wrong decorations, crying children. But sure, I am the one who is too controlling.

Nudge: "What held you back from responding?"

Dropped off three containers of biryani to the Sharma family down the street. Their son just had surgery and the mother looked like she had not slept in days. She cried when she saw me at the door. This is what it is all about, really. Showing up when people need you.

Nudge: "How did it feel when she cried?"

Response: Honestly, it reminded me of when Amma had her first stroke and neighbors brought us food for weeks. You never forget who showed up. I want to be that person for others. Sometimes I think this is the only thing I am truly good at.

Priya came home crying from school because her friend group excluded her from a birthday party. I spent an hour consoling her but the whole time I kept thinking about that committee meeting. Is this what it feels like? To be pushed out? Too tired to think about this more.

Nudge: "Are you thinking about yourself or Priya?"

Response: Priya, obviously. She is thirteen and everything feels like the end of the world at that age. I am just tired.

Ravi's sister called asking if we can host his parents for six weeks instead of two this summer. She has a work trip to Singapore that "cannot be rescheduled." I said yes before even thinking about it. What else could I say?

Now I am mentally reorganizing everything. Priya's room will have to be cleared for them. I will need to cook separate low-sodium meals for Appa. The bathroom schedule becomes a nightmare with seven people. But Lakshmi made it sound like I was the only solution, and I suppose I am.

Nudge: "Did you want to say something else?"

Response: Wanting and doing are different things. Lakshmi works, I do not. She has meetings in Singapore, I have school drop-offs. The math is simple. But yes, fine, I wanted to say "let me check with Ravi first" like a normal person. Instead I just absorbed it like always.

Nothing happened today, just the usual. But I have been thinking about something my cousin Anita said when she visited last month. She asked why I "gave up" my career to stay home, as if I am sitting here watching television all day. I manage a household, a family, a community. I coordinate schedules for five people, handle three different school systems' requirements, manage all the finances, plan every festival, and support whoever needs me.

Ravi could not find his passport last week. Who found it in twenty minutes? Me. Priya needed a costume for her Bharatanatyam recital. Who stayed up sewing until midnight? Me. Vikram's college applications needed someone to proofread seven essays. Me.

But because there is no salary, it is not real work. Because I do not have a LinkedIn profile anymore, I have "given up." Anita has a corner office and takes two vacations a year. Good for her. She also calls her mother once a month and sends birthday cards late. I am not saying one is better. I am just saying they are different, and I am tired of having to defend my choice.

Committee met again. I volunteered to step back from leading the Navratri planning. Let someone else handle the vendor contracts and volunteer schedules. Meena aunty looked surprised. Everyone did, actually.

Nudge: "How did it feel to step back?"

Made chai for myself this afternoon and sat on the balcony for fifteen minutes doing nothing. The house was quiet. Just me and the crows arguing in the mango tree. Cannot remember the last time I did this.

Nudge: "What made today different?"

Vikram called from university just to talk. Not because he needed money or help with something. Just to tell me about his professor who reminds him of his grandfather, and about the dosa place he found near campus. We talked for forty-five minutes.

I think I did something right with him. He is kind. He notices people. Last month he organized a food drive at his dorm because he saw international students struggling during spring break when the dining hall closed. He did not tell me about it - his roommate's mother mentioned it. That is the part that makes me proudest. He does not do it for recognition.

Nudge: "What do you think shaped him that way?"

Response: I wish I could take credit but honestly, I think he just watched. Watched me drop off food to the Sharmas. Watched his grandmother live with us for three years. Watched Ravi stay late at office but still make every recital and cricket match. Children learn what they see, not what you tell them.

Second entry today. In-laws arrived this evening, four days early. Lakshmi's flight got moved up. I had just finished cleaning but the guest room mattress has not been aired. Amma looked at the sheets and I know she noticed. Too tired to care right now.

Amma has been here four days and I am already losing my mind. She reorganized my spice cabinet. MY spice cabinet that I have arranged the same way for fifteen years. She said she was "helping" because it was "inefficient." I have managed three children, a husband, a house, and an entire temple committee with that "inefficient" cabinet.

I did not say anything. What would be the point? She is seventy-two and she is a guest. But Ravi saw my face when I opened the cabinet door and he just shrugged. Shrugged! As if his mother rearranging my kitchen is completely normal and I should just accept it.

The worst part is that she is not wrong. Her arrangement probably is more efficient. But that is not the point. The point is that this is my home, my space, the one domain where I should have some say. But apparently even here I am meant to just smile and adjust. I made everyone's favorite dinner anyway because what else am I going to do, let everyone starve over cumin placement?

Nudge: "What would having a say look like?"

Response: It does not matter. Four more weeks and then things go back to normal. I just need to survive until then.

Priya asked me today why I always have to be "in charge of everything." She was annoyed because I reminded her three times about her homework. Three times! Because she was on her phone and the deadline was in two hours. But apparently I am controlling.

I told her that someone has to keep track of things. She rolled her eyes and said "you could just let me fail once." What kind of mother lets her child fail on purpose? I did not hover over Vikram and look how he turned out - responsible, organized, successful. But Priya is different. She needs more reminders. If I do not push her, she will coast.

Ravi thinks I should "give her space." Easy for him to say. He is at office twelve hours a day. He does not see the daily negotiation of getting this child to do anything. When she fails a test, no one will ask where her father was. They will ask what I was doing.

But maybe everyone is right and I am the problem. Maybe I should just stop caring. Let the laundry pile up, let the homework go undone, let the spice cabinet stay however Amma wants it. Then we will see how things run.

Nudge: "What would happen if you did stop?"

Response: Chaos. Or maybe nothing. Maybe everything would be fine and I would realize I have been making myself essential when I am actually just in the way. That thought is worse than the chaos, honestly.

Amma and I had an argument today. Or not an argument exactly - she does not argue. She just makes observations. "In my house we always did it this way." "You work so hard, beta, but maybe if you organized differently..." "Priya seems stressed, have you thought about letting her breathe?"

I finally said something. I told her that I appreciate her concern but this is my house and my family and I have been managing fine for twenty years. She got very quiet and went to her room. Ravi says I was too harsh. Maybe. But she has been here two weeks and every day there is a new suggestion, a new correction, a new way I am falling short.

The thing is, she ran her own household for forty years. I understand the need to feel useful, to have a role. When she moved in with Lakshmi she lost all of that. So she comes here and tries to find her place by improving mine. I see the pattern. I am not blind to it.

But seeing the pattern does not make it easier to live with. And I keep wondering - is this my future? Will I be the mother-in-law who cannot stop fixing things because fixing things is all I know how to do? Will Priya write journal entries about me reorganizing her kitchen?

Nudge: "What would you want to be remembered for?"

Response: Not for being right. Not for having the most organized spice cabinet or the best-run committee. I want Vikram and Priya and even little Arun to remember that I showed up. That when things fell apart, I was there. That I fed people not because I needed to control the menu but because I believe you cannot love someone properly if you let them go hungry. That is why I do all of this. I think I forgot that somewhere along the way.

Mummyji made a comment at lunch about the indigo stains on my fingernails. She didn't say anything directly, just that thing she does where she looks at my hands and then looks at the dal and raises her eyebrows. Ravi was right there and said nothing. I know he thinks the dyeing thing is fine, he's told me so, but he won't say it in front of her. So I'm the one who looks like I'm neglecting things.

The thing is, the kitchen was spotless. Lunch was on time. Arjun's tiffin was packed before school. I don't know what more she wants from me. Or maybe I do know and I just don't want to give it, which is this complete surrender of any time that's mine. Like the house should swallow me whole and I should be grateful for it.

I scrubbed the indigo off after lunch. Took ten minutes with lemon and baking soda. I shouldn't have had to do that. But I did it anyway and I'm not sure what that says about me.

Nudge: "Why did you scrub them off?"

Response: Because it was easier than sitting through another silent dinner with her staring at my hands. I pick my fights and that one wasn't worth it. But it felt like erasing something.

Arjun's parent-teacher meeting today. The class teacher mentioned he draws during free period instead of playing cricket with the other boys and asked if everything is okay at home. I wanted to say, yes, everything is fine, my son likes drawing, why is that a problem? But I smiled and nodded and said I'd encourage him to be more social.

Came home and told Ravi about it and he said maybe Arjun should join a sport, that it would be good for him. And I thought, here we go. Everyone has an opinion about what the boy should be doing except the boy himself.

Nudge: "What stopped you from saying that to the teacher?"

Response: I don't know. You just don't, do you? Not in that setting.

Made aloo paratha for breakfast the way Appa used to, with extra ajwain in the dough. The smell filled the whole kitchen and for a second I was back in our old house in Mysore, watching him roll them out, flour everywhere.

Spent the whole morning at the Diwali mela helping set up the community puja. I did the rangoli at the entrance the way my grandmother taught me, rice flour with turmeric borders, none of the acrylic paint shortcuts the younger crowd uses. Three women came up and asked me to show them how. Felt good.

Later, back home, I worked on a new indigo resist pattern. Nobody was around to comment on it. Ravi had taken Arjun and Vihaan to the cricket match. Two hours of silence and my hands doing what they wanted to do. I didn't even check my phone.

Nudge: "Which part of the day stuck with you more?"

Mummyji wants me to start teaching Vihaan the Vishnu Sahasranama. He's seven. She says she learned it by six. I said I'd start next week, which I will, because it's important, Appa made sure I knew it by heart and I'm glad he did. But there's this edge to the way she asks, like she's testing whether I'm doing enough.

Ravi's cousin Priya called in the evening. She started a small catering business last year and wanted to know if I'd be interested in supplying hand-dyed table linens. I said I'd think about it. Didn't mention it to anyone at home yet.

Nudge: "Are you going to tell Ravi about Priya's offer?"

Sat down this morning to teach Vihaan the first twenty verses. He's quicker than I expected. He kept asking what the words meant and I found myself explaining them properly, not just the rote way I was taught but actually talking about what each name describes. He asked why Vishnu has so many names and I said because one name is never enough for something that big. He seemed satisfied with that.

It reminded me of sitting with Appa in the puja room, early mornings, the smell of camphor and sandalwood paste. He was patient. He never rushed me or made it feel like a chore. I want to give Vihaan that same feeling, not the pressure of getting it right, but the warmth of sitting together with something that matters. The sense that this is ours, this thread that connects us backward through generations, something nobody can take away or schedule over.

Mummyji passed by the room and listened for a while and actually smiled. She didn't say anything, didn't correct his pronunciation or mine. She just stood there and smiled. That was enough for today.

Spent the afternoon cataloguing my dye samples. I have fourteen shades of indigo now, ranging from almost-grey to a deep navy that I got by double-dipping with a fermentation vat I built from a recipe in an old Kalamkari manual. Labelled each one with the mordant used, the dip count, the fabric type. Cotton takes differently than silk, and I'm getting more consistent results with the cotton muslin I sourced from a weaver in Solapur.

Priya messaged again about the linens. She has a wedding order for February. Twelve table runners, twenty napkins. The money would be decent. I told her I need to think about scale. My terrace setup can handle maybe four meters of fabric at a time. I'd need to figure out drying space and timing around the kids' schedules.

Vihaan came up to the terrace after his homework and asked if he could dip his hand in the vat. I let him. His palm came out blue and he ran downstairs to show everyone. Mummyji was not pleased.

Nudge: "So are you doing it?"

Response: Haven't decided. Need to work out the logistics first.

Told Ravi about Priya's proposal over chai after the boys went to bed. He was more supportive than I expected. He said if I wanted to try it, he'd help me figure out the money side. He even suggested converting part of the back store room into a workspace. I hadn't thought about that.

What surprised me was my own hesitation. He was offering exactly what I thought I wanted, backing, space, permission. But when it was right there in front of me I felt this pull backward, like, who am I to run a business? My mother didn't. Mummyji didn't. All the women I grew up watching ran homes, not enterprises. And I love running this home. That's the part that gets lost when people hear I want to do something else. I don't want to leave, I want to also.

Nudge: "What does 'also' actually look like day to day?"

Response: Mornings with the boys, school drop-off, puja, cooking. Afternoons when they're in school or doing homework, that's my window. Three, maybe four hours. It's not a lot but it's mine. I just need people to stop treating those hours like leftover time that belongs to the house.

Mummyji brought up the store room idea at dinner, in front of Ravi's parents who were visiting. She said something like, "Meera is thinking of opening a factory in the house." She was smiling but it was the wrong kind of smile. Ravi's father asked what she was talking about and I had to explain the dyeing and the linen idea in front of everyone while trying not to sound like I was defending myself.

Ravi stepped in eventually and said it was just a small project. The word "small" stuck. I know he was trying to help but it felt like he was shrinking it to make it palatable. I didn't say anything after that. Finished dinner. Cleared the plates. Went upstairs.

Can't sleep. Ravi's parents left after dinner and everyone is being normal now but I keep replaying the table conversation. The worst part isn't what Mummyji said, it's that nobody asked me a real question about it. Nobody said, what kind of fabric, what's the process, how did you learn. They only wanted to know if it would interfere with anything.

I keep thinking about Appa's workshop in Mysore. He repaired watches. Tiny, patient, precise work. Nobody ever questioned whether he should be doing it. It was just what he did. I want that. Not the permission, not the approval. Just the quiet assumption that of course I do this, it's mine.

Nudge: "What would Appa say about the indigo?"

Took the boys to the Ganesh temple this morning for the monthly abhishekam. Vihaan recited the first twenty verses of the Sahasranama on the auto ride there, unprompted. I didn't even know he'd been practising on his own. The priest noticed and said something to me about keeping these things alive. I felt proud but also this strange defensiveness, like I needed to prove I wasn't only about the dyeing, that I still carry all of this too.

Why do I feel like I have to choose? Like every hour I spend with the indigo is an hour stolen from something else. Nobody told Ravi he has to choose between his consulting work and being a father. It's just assumed he can be both.

Nudge: "Who's actually making you choose?"

Called Priya back tonight and said yes to the February order. Twelve runners, twenty napkins, indigo with a kalamkari-inspired border block I've been working on. She sounded happy. I felt terrified and then calm, in that order, like jumping into cold water.

Haven't told Mummyji yet. Told Ravi, who said congratulations and then asked if I needed help setting up the store room this weekend. I said yes. Vihaan overheard and asked if he could help with the blue. I said yes to that too. I don't know if I'm doing the right thing. But I know I'm doing my thing, and right now that has to be enough.

Long day of lesson planning for the new term. Three back-to-back committee meetings about the revised syllabus, and I still have Arjun's mock test papers to review tonight. Just want to sit with my chai and not think about grade distributions for five minutes.

Writing again because something from the afternoon meeting is bothering me. The principal floated the idea of restructuring the science department — merging physics and chemistry into combined sections for the lower forms. She said it would "optimize staffing." I've been the dedicated physics teacher since 2003.

Nothing is decided yet, apparently. But Sunita from chemistry seemed to already know about it before the meeting. I kept my face neutral but my stomach dropped. Twenty-two years of building this department's board results, and now they want to fold it into something generic. I need to find out what's actually being proposed before I react.

Nudge: "What specifically felt wrong about Sunita already knowing?"

Arjun got his mock results back — 94th percentile in physics, 89th overall. I know I shouldn't take credit but I've been drilling him on wave mechanics every evening for two months. This is what it looks like when the work actually pays off.

Nudge: "Whose accomplishment does that feel like — his or yours?"

Response: Both, honestly. His effort, my method.

Parent-teacher conferences all week. Same questions every year — will my child pass, what rank can they get, can you give extra coaching. Ravi's mother brought sweets to thank me for his board results. Small thing but it matters.

A colleague mentioned she's applying to an international school in Bangalore — nearly double the salary. For a moment I caught myself looking it up. Then I thought about uprooting Arjun right before his entrance exams, and Priya's board year. The timing is impossible, but I wonder if I use the children's schedules as an excuse not to take chances.

The restructuring discussion came up again in staff meeting. This time with a proper proposal document. It's not as bad as I feared — physics stays as a standalone subject for the upper forms, but lower forms will have integrated science. What bothered me last time was the uncertainty, not knowing what was coming. Now that I can see the actual plan, I can work with it.

I spoke up during the meeting and suggested that the integrated syllabus should still map to the board examination topics so students aren't disadvantaged when they specialize. The principal noted it down. Two other teachers came to me after and said they were glad someone raised it. That felt right — using my experience to shape the outcome rather than just worrying about it.

Ramesh said I should have pushed harder, demanded that physics remain separate at all levels. But I know how these things work. You push too hard and you get sidelined from the committee entirely. Better to be at the table with a reasonable suggestion than shouting from outside. Twenty-two years has taught me at least that much.

Nudge: "You mentioned feeling right about shaping the outcome — what would 'wrong' have looked like?"

Response: Sitting quiet and hoping someone else would fix it.

Deepak and I had one of those late-night conversations after the children went to sleep. He mentioned that his bank is offering voluntary retirement packages for employees over 50. He's 52. He said he's not considering it, but the way he brought it up — casually, while reading the newspaper — made me think he's been thinking about it more than he let on.

If he took it, the pension would cover basics but we'd lose the medical benefits and the housing allowance. I ran the numbers in my head before he'd even finished talking. I do this — immediately calculate what could go wrong instead of just listening. He probably just wanted to talk about it, and I turned it into a risk assessment.

Nudge: "What would happen if you just listened first?"

New term properly started. The integrated science trial begins next week for the lower forms and I'm one of three teachers overseeing the transition. More work, obviously, but also more visibility with the administration. The principal specifically asked me to lead the physics component mapping, which is essentially acknowledging that the department runs on my curriculum design.

Arjun has his coaching institute sessions ramping up — three evenings a week now plus full Saturdays. I can't help him as much with the entrance prep anymore because the syllabus has moved beyond what I drill at home. Hired a tutor for the mathematics portions. It's expensive but this is not the place to cut corners. Priya seems fine, quietly doing her work, not demanding attention. I should probably check in with her more but the days just fill up.

The staffroom chatter is about the annual teacher evaluation coming up in October. Last year I got "exceeds expectations" which put me in line for the senior teacher increment. Need to make sure the integrated science pilot goes well — that's going to be on the evaluation.

Priya came to me after dinner and asked if I could look at her biology project. I almost said "after I finish marking" but something made me stop. Put the papers down and sat with her for an hour. Her project is actually quite clever — comparing germination rates under different water pH levels. She'd done all the data collection herself without asking for help.

I realized I've been so focused on Arjun's entrance preparation because it feels urgent and measurable — there's a rank, a cutoff, a clear outcome. Priya's work doesn't have that same urgency so it keeps sliding down the list. But sitting with her tonight, watching her explain her methodology, I felt something I haven't felt in weeks. Just pride without calculation. No percentile attached.

She asked if I thought she could pursue research someday. I told her absolutely. Deepak joined us for the last ten minutes and it was the most relaxed the house has felt since August. Marking can wait until morning. I'll set the alarm for five.

Got an email from the international school my colleague mentioned. They're still hiring for physics. I read the entire job posting before deleting it. Told myself it was just curiosity but I bookmarked the school's website.

Nudge: "What did you picture while reading the posting?"

Response: Doesn't matter, the timing won't work.

Evaluation prep meeting today. Reviewed my portfolio — student pass rates up 4% year-on-year, the integrated science pilot is on track, three students from my batch qualified for the state science olympiad. Good numbers. I know how to build a case.

Funny thing is, after putting together all the evidence of what I've done this year, the moment that sticks with me most is sitting with Priya and her germination experiment. None of that goes into the portfolio. The things that get recognized and the things that actually feel meaningful don't always line up, and I've spent most of my career pretending they do.

Still, the evaluation matters. The increment matters. The children's fees, the tutoring costs, Arjun's coaching — it all adds up. I can hold both things at once: knowing that the numbers don't capture everything, and also knowing that I need those numbers to take care of what matters. The trick is not forgetting which one is actually the point.

Nudge: "Which one is actually the point?"

Response: Taking care of them. The rest is how I get there.

Another steering committee meeting where half the room was on their phones. I run a tight agenda and still can't get people to care about infrastructure decisions that will haunt us for years. Nobody wants to own anything hard anymore.

Nudge: "What does it cost you when they check out like that?"

Response: It makes me wonder why I bother holding the line if nobody else will.

Found out today they picked Raj for the VP role. The announcement went out at 2pm in a company-wide email. I heard about it from my skip-level before that, which I guess was supposed to soften the blow. Raj has been here six years. I have been here nineteen.

I am not saying he is unqualified. He ran the platform migration last year and it went well. But I have been running the reliability council since before he joined the company. I built the incident response process from nothing. Three directors told me privately that they assumed the role was mine. So either those conversations meant nothing or something changed that nobody told me about.

Had a one-on-one with my manager afterward. She said the decision came down to "organizational energy" and "forward-looking vision." I asked what that meant concretely and she pivoted to talking about how much they value my technical depth. That phrase again. Technical depth. It is starting to feel like a box they put you in when they do not see you leading.

Nudge: "Those three directors who assumed it was yours -- did any of them advocate for you?"

Response: I actually do not know. I assumed they did but maybe assuming was the whole problem.
Spent most of Saturday reviewing the Q3 reliability targets. Updated my tracking sheet for the team. Chen is slipping again, two missed deadlines this month.

Cannot stop thinking about the VP thing. Told myself I was over it but then I saw Raj's new title in the org chart and my chest got tight. Thirty years and I am still a "senior individual contributor." Hate that I care this much.

Nudge: "You said you hate that you care. What would it look like if you didn't?"

Response: Honestly I have no idea. I do not know who I am if I stop keeping score.

Weird thing happened at the barbecue yesterday. Tom from next door, he retired from Boeing last year, he was talking about building a deck. Not a fancy deck, just a simple one off the back of his kitchen. And he was describing the process of picking the lumber, getting the permits, measuring everything twice, and I realized I was genuinely envious. Not of the deck. Of the way he talked about it. Like it mattered to him for no reason other than he wanted to do it.

I have not built anything with my hands in years. Used to do woodworking in my twenties before the career took off. I still have my dad's old chisels in the garage somewhere, wrapped in an oilcloth. I think about them sometimes.

After the barbecue I went home and sat in the garage for a while. Found the chisels. They were right where I left them, maybe eight years ago. The handles are still good. I did not do anything with them, just held one and felt the weight. Karen came out and asked what I was doing and I said "nothing" which I guess was technically true. But it did not feel like nothing.

Long week. Raj scheduled a "vision alignment" meeting for the whole engineering org and I had to sit through it smiling. He is reorganizing the reliability council under his new structure which means my committee now reports up through him. Three years of building that group and now I am presenting to someone I used to mentor.

Karen noticed I have been staying up late. She asked if I was okay and I said I was fine, just busy. She did not push it. Part of me wanted her to push it.

Nudge: "Why not just tell Karen what's actually going on?"

Spent Sunday morning updating my LinkedIn profile. Added a few bullet points about the incident response framework and the reliability council. Looked at job postings out of curiosity. Principal engineer roles at three companies. Two of them pay more than what I make now.

Did not apply to any of them. Just screenshot the listings and saved them to a folder on my desktop called "Options." Have not decided if that is planning or procrastination.

Nudge: "That folder -- when do you think you will actually open it again?"

Had a skip-level with the CTO today. She asked me to lead a new cross-functional task force on AI readiness. Big scope, high visibility, reports directly to her. On paper it is exactly what I have been wanting. Direct line to the top, chance to shape strategy.

But I keep thinking about why now. Two weeks after passing me over they hand me a consolation project. Raj probably suggested it. I said yes because saying no would look petty. Not sure if I actually want to do it or if I just cannot stand to be seen turning down something important.

Took a half day and went to the hardware store. Bought some walnut stock and a new set of clamps. Nothing serious, just enough to try a small cutting board. Felt strange buying something that has nothing to do with work or credentials or org charts.

Spent the afternoon in the garage planing the wood down. My technique is rusty but the motions came back faster than I expected. I was out there for three hours and did not check my phone once. I cannot remember the last time that happened.

Nudge: "Three hours without your phone. What was going through your head out there?"

Response: Mostly just the grain direction and whether my measurements were right. That was the whole point I think. Just the thing in front of me.

First AI task force meeting was today. I spent all weekend preparing a charter document, twelve pages, frameworks, timelines, resource asks. I presented it and the CTO said "this is thorough" which I used to take as a compliment but now I wonder if it means "too much." She asked if we could "move faster and iterate." That phrase.

Raj sat in on the meeting. He did not say much but he was nodding along like he was evaluating me. Maybe I am reading into it. Probably I am reading into it. But I have been in enough rooms to know when someone is there to observe rather than participate.

Came home and Karen had dinner ready which was nice. She asked about my day and I gave her the short version. She said it sounded like a good opportunity. I agreed. We watched something on TV and I fell asleep on the couch before it ended. She put a blanket on me. I woke up at 2am still on the couch and went upstairs and she was already asleep. These are the kinds of things that keep happening and I am not sure either of us is noticing how much space there is between us now.

Nudge: "That blanket she put on you -- when did you two stop talking about the hard stuff?"

Response: We talk. Just not about the things that would require either of us to change something.

Finished the cutting board. Walnut with maple inlay, nothing fancy but the joints are tight. Rubbed it down with mineral oil and it looks good. Brought it inside and set it on the counter.

Karen picked it up and turned it over a few times. She said "You made this?" and I said yeah. She put it down and said "It's really beautiful, Gerry." Something about the way she said my name. Have not heard her say it like that in a long time.

Second task force meeting went better. I cut the charter down to four pages and proposed a two-week sprint to prototype something instead of planning for another month. The CTO seemed pleased. Raj was not there this time which helped. I could just run the room without wondering if I was being graded.

Chen came to me after the meeting and said he wants to transfer onto the task force. I was surprised. He has been underperforming on my reliability team but maybe that is because he does not care about reliability work. Maybe I have been measuring him against the wrong thing. I told him I would think about it but I think I am going to say yes.

Ordered more walnut and some cherry this time. Thinking about making a small side table next. Karen asked if she could watch me work in the garage sometime and I said sure. We are going to Portland together next weekend. Her idea but I said yes without hesitating which is new. The Options folder is still on my desktop. I have not opened it in two weeks. Not sure what that means yet but it does not bother me the way it probably should.

The coordinadora pulled me aside after class and told me my reading circle format doesn't align with the ministry's new standardized framework. She wants me to switch to the workbook model by next week. I said I'd look into it but I'm not changing what's working.

Nudge: "What happens when next week comes and you haven't switched?"

People keep acting like I'm being difficult but I sat through two years of watching kids at the private school fill out comprehension worksheets with dead eyes. My students here actually talk about the books we read. They argue about characters. Last week Sofia, who barely spoke in September, stood up and defended her interpretation of the ending of the story to the whole class. That doesn't come from a workbook.

I talked to the coordinadora again today and told her straight up that I'd document my students' progress however she needs but I won't gut the program to fit a checkbox. She didn't love it. But she didn't say no either. My mom thinks I should just comply and keep my head down, which is rich coming from someone who argued with every teacher I ever had.

Nudge: "Your mom told you to comply. How did that land?"

Response: It stung more than it should have. She's the reason I push back on things in the first place.

Took Camilo through practice problems for the ICFES all Saturday morning. He's getting better at the reading comprehension sections, still shaky on the quantitative reasoning but that's not really my wheelhouse. I set him up with a YouTube channel that breaks it down step by step and told him to message me if he gets stuck. His mom, my tia Elena, called from Medellin to thank me, which was nice.

Went to abuela's in the afternoon. She'd made sancocho even though it was just the two of us, enough for ten people as usual. We watched a novela together and I fixed the latch on her back door that's been loose for months. She seems okay. A little quieter than normal maybe, but she always gets like that around the anniversary of abuelo's passing.

Long day. Parent meetings ran until six and then I stayed to reorganize the classroom library because nobody else was going to. Camilo called with a question about a practice test but I was too fried to be useful so I told him we'd go over it this weekend.

Nudge: "You couldn't help Camilo tonight. Does that bother you?"

Response: Yeah, a little. He doesn't ask unless he's actually stuck.

Got an email from the district office about a new mandate requiring all language arts teachers to submit weekly lesson plans using their template. The template is basically a fill-in-the-blank thing with pre-set learning objectives and approved activities. I forwarded it to my colleague Patricia and she just shrugged and said she'd start using it. I get it, she has two kids and no energy to fight bureaucracy. But I can't just do that.

The thing is, I know how this looks. I know people see me as the one who always has to do things her own way, the difficult teacher, the one who won't just go along. And maybe there's some truth in it. But I've also seen what happens when you hand over every decision about your classroom to someone who's never met your students. The kids in Ciudad Bolivar don't need another generic worksheet. They need someone paying attention to what actually reaches them.

I'm going to keep using my own plans and submit the template version as a formality. I've done this before. It's more work but I'd rather do double the planning than teach something I don't believe in.

Nudge: "You said maybe there's truth in being the difficult one. What's that like?"

Response: Lonely sometimes. Patricia used to push back with me but she stopped. I don't blame her but I notice it.

Second time writing today because I can't stop thinking about something that happened after school. One of my students, Diego, waited around after everyone left and asked me if I could help him write a letter to his dad who's working in Chile. He said his mom doesn't have time and he doesn't know how to start. So we sat there for forty minutes and I helped him figure out what he wanted to say. He wanted to tell his dad about a soccer game he played in and about a dog that follows him home from school every day.

It was one of those moments where you remember why you're doing this. Not the curriculum fights or the template emails or the district mandates. Just a kid who needed someone to sit with him and say yeah, that's worth writing down, your dad would want to hear about the dog.

I keep thinking about how many kids like Diego just need someone to stop and listen for half an hour. And then I think about how stretched thin I already am and how I told Camilo I'd help him this weekend and I promised abuela I'd come by Sunday and I still haven't finished planning next week's classes because I was busy making the fake template version for the district. There's only so much of me to go around.

Spent the morning with Camilo going through the quantitative reasoning section. He's definitely improving. We worked through the problems he'd flagged and he got about seventy percent right on his own before I stepped in. Told him that's real progress and he actually smiled, which he doesn't do much these days since he's so stressed about the exam. His mom is counting on him getting into a public university because they can't afford private tuition, so the pressure is real.

After that I went to abuela's. She was in better spirits today. She'd been to the market and bought ingredients for arepas de choclo, so we made them together. She told me stories about when she and abuelo first came to Bogota from the campo and how they used to make arepas on a clay stove. I've heard the stories before but I don't mind. She seems to need to tell them.

On the bus home I sketched out a rough plan for next week's reading unit. I'm going to have the kids write letters, inspired by Diego. They'll pick someone they want to tell something to and we'll work on voice and structure through that. It fits the ministry objectives if you squint at it the right way, and it's actually something the kids will care about.

Nudge: "Diego's letter became a whole unit. Does that happen a lot?"

Response: More than I realize probably. The best ideas come from paying attention to what the kids actually need, not from a planning document.

Tia Elena called tonight and said Camilo bombed his practice test today, the one right after our session. She wasn't blaming me but it felt like it. I keep wondering if I'm actually helping him or just making myself feel useful.

Nudge: "Making yourself feel useful — what made you put it that way?"

Response: I don't know, I'm just tired. He'll be fine, he's smart enough.

The meeting with Diego didn't go how I planned. He sat there scrolling through my routing proposal on his laptop, barely looking up, and when he finally said something it was about the formatting. The formatting. Three months of pulling delivery data, testing alternate corridors, calling warehouse contacts in Medellin -- and he wants to talk about fonts.

I should have pushed back harder. Instead I just nodded and said I'd revise it. The whole walk back to my desk I was already rewriting my argument in my head, all the things I should have said. The numbers speak for themselves. Our current route through Bucaramanga adds two days and the clients in the southern corridor keep complaining.

Part of me wonders if I backed down because he's been here fifteen years and I've been here eleven months. That shouldn't matter when the data is right there. But I sat quiet like it did matter, and now I'm stuck doing cosmetic edits on something that should already be approved.

Nudge: "Why do you think you went quiet?"

Still at the office. Everyone else left at six but I'm going through the client satisfaction numbers from Q4 because nobody else is going to do it. Found three accounts where response times slipped below target because the team was running the old dispatch sequence I already flagged two months ago. Two months.

My mom called while I was pulling the spreadsheets. I let it go to voicemail. She'll ask why I'm still at work, then she'll ask when I'm coming home for New Year's, and I don't have answers to either one right now.

Nudge: "Are you going home for New Year's?"

Response: Probably not. There's too much to clean up here before January. She'll understand. She usually does.

Spent New Year's Eve at Lucia's apartment with a few people from the office. Nothing big -- arepas, aguardiente, the countdown on the TV. I didn't check my phone once the whole night, which felt strange at first but I stopped noticing after a while. Lucia made fun of me for not knowing any of the songs on the playlist.
