On January first I went for a run along the ciclovia and came back with an idea for restructuring the southern corridor drops. Not the full routing plan Diego shut down -- something smaller, a pilot with just the Cali deliveries. If I can show a cost reduction on one leg, the rest of the argument builds itself. Nobody has to approve a pilot. I can just run it with the three drivers who already told me the current route doesn't make sense.

I drafted the pilot scope on my laptop sitting on Lucia's couch while everyone was watching a movie. Felt good to have something concrete again instead of waiting for permission. I want to own how this unfolds, start to finish.

The Cali pilot is running. Three drivers, rerouted through Palmira instead of the standard loop. I didn't tell Diego. I told the drivers it was a test week and tracked the first four deliveries myself from the dashboard. Average time dropped by six hours per run. Six hours. That's not a font issue.

I built a one-page summary with the numbers and sent it to the full operations chat, not just Diego. Maybe that was aggressive. Lucia said I should have gone through him first, and she's probably right in terms of politics. But if I go through him it sits in his inbox for two weeks and nothing changes. I've watched that happen before.

He hasn't responded yet. Part of me is nervous but mostly I feel like I did the thing I actually wanted to do instead of waiting around editing slide decks. If the numbers hold through Friday, the argument is over. I want this to stand on the work, not on who approved it.

Diego pulled me into his office yesterday and I thought he was going to tear into me about the pilot but he just asked for the full data set. No commentary, no lecture, just "send me the spreadsheet." I sent it within the hour. He forwarded it to the VP of ops in Mexico City. I know because the VP emailed me directly asking for a call next week.

That call could be anything. Could be a reprimand, could be an expansion. Either way the work is out there now and it's mine. I keep refreshing my email like that's going to make the calendar invite appear faster. Need to stop doing that.

Nudge: "What are you hoping she says?"

Response: I don't know. Something that makes the last three months feel like they counted.

Call with the VP happened. Forty-five minutes. She asked detailed questions about methodology, cost projections, scalability to other corridors. She knew what she was talking about, which made it feel real -- not like presenting to someone who just wants to check a box.

At the end she said she wants me to present to the regional leadership group in February. A full presentation, not a one-pager. My stomach dropped when she said it, not because I'm scared but because I've never had that kind of stage before and I want it to go perfectly. I already know I'm going to over-prepare.

Called my mom after to tell her. She said "mija, eat something, you sound wired." She's not wrong. I had an empanada for lunch and nothing since. But this is the thing I've been pushing for since I got here and it's actually happening. Not because someone handed it to me -- because I built it and put it out there myself.

Nudge: "What does a perfect presentation look like to you?"

Response: Numbers that nobody can argue with, questions I already have answers for, and walking out knowing they couldn't have gotten that analysis from anyone else on the team.

Stayed up until three working on the deck. Caught myself copying Diego's formatting from an old presentation because I thought it would make the leadership group take it more seriously. Closed the file and went to bed feeling off about it.

Lucia told me today that some people on the team think I'm "trying to skip levels." She said it like she was doing me a favor by telling me, and maybe she was, but it stung. I've been busting myself on this project and the fact that doing good work gets read as political maneuvering makes me want to throw my laptop into the Magdalena.

I asked her who said it and she wouldn't tell me. Fine. But now I'm sitting here wondering if I should tone it down for the February presentation or just go harder. Toning it down feels like giving up ground I earned. Going harder might prove their point. I don't know.

Finished the presentation draft this morning. Fourteen slides, every number sourced, three scenario projections. I ran through it out loud twice in my apartment and timed it at eighteen minutes, which leaves room for questions. My neighbor probably thinks I'm losing it.

Went to my abuela's for almuerzo after. She made sancocho and didn't ask about work at all, just told me about her friend Doris who's fighting with her landlord. Sat there eating soup and listening and realized I hadn't thought about the presentation for almost two hours. That's probably the longest I've gone without thinking about it in weeks.

Nudge: "How did the two hours feel?"

Response: Good. Like my brain got to stop running for a bit. I forgot what that was like.

Diego sent me "feedback" on the presentation draft. Half of it is adding his name to the second slide as a co-contributor. He didn't contribute. He sat on my original proposal for weeks and only engaged after I went around him. Now he wants credit on the version that works.

I know the smart move is probably to add his name and keep the peace. Everyone keeps telling me to "play the game." But I didn't build this by playing the game. I built it by ignoring the game and doing the actual work. If I put his name on it, what am I even presenting? Someone else's stamp on my project. I'm going to push back. Maybe that's stupid. I don't care.

Nudge: "Do you actually not care?"

Presentation went well. The VP asked me to lead a corridor optimization working group across three countries. Diego shook my hand after and didn't mention the co-credit thing. I slept eleven hours last night and I'm still tired, but it's the kind of tired where you know you did the thing.

My advisor asked today why I chose the topic of hospitality rituals in Levantine households when there are "more contemporary angles" I could take. I told her it was methodologically sound and left it at that. I don't owe anyone an explanation for why this subject matters to me. She's Swedish. She studies migration patterns. The way my grandmother set a table is not something she would understand, and I'm not interested in making it legible to her.

Came home and made musakhan from scratch, the whole process — rolling the dough, roasting the sumac onions until they were dark and sweet. Ate it alone at the kitchen table while my mother watched her show in the other room. The apartment smelled right. That was enough of an answer for one day.

Nudge: "You said you don't owe an explanation. But you came home and spent hours making your grandmother's dish. What was that about?"

Response: I like cooking. It's not that deep. The musakhan was good and I hadn't made it in a while. Sometimes dinner is just dinner.
Spent twelve hours in the library and barely got through two chapters of my lit review. My back hurts, my eyes hurt, and I keep wondering what possessed me to do this at forty-nine. Going to bed.

My brother Faisal made a comment at Friday lunch about how I'm "still playing student" while his sons are already building careers. His eldest is twenty-six and works at a bank. I smiled and passed the hummus. What I wanted to say was that I spent twenty years running hotel operations so our family name meant something in the hospitality industry, and now I'm doing something for myself, and that should be allowed.

But it wouldn't have been the right moment. My mother was sitting right there, and she gets anxious when Faisal and I disagree. So I stayed quiet, helped clear the plates, and drove home listening to Fairuz with the windows down. The breeze off the hills felt good. I don't need Faisal's approval. I know what I'm doing and why.

Nudge: "What would have happened if you'd actually said it?"

Response: My mother would have gone quiet. Faisal would have gotten louder. His wife Rania would have changed the subject to the children, which she always does. And on the drive home I would have felt worse, not better, because I'd have disrupted the one gathering that keeps us together. The lunch matters more than being right. That's something Faisal wouldn't understand because he's never had to choose.

Can't stop thinking about what Faisal said. Opened a bottle of that Jordanian red wine I've been saving and I'm sitting on the balcony. My mother would not approve of the wine but she went to bed early and what she doesn't see doesn't trouble her.

Had a strange afternoon. After seminar I went to the old souk downtown instead of going home to write. I just walked. Bought a bag of roasted pistachios from the same vendor my father used to take me to when I was a boy. The man running it now is probably the vendor's grandson, same spot, same brass scale. I sat on a bench near the mosque and ate them slowly, one by one, listening to the call to prayer echo off the stone.

I haven't prayed regularly in years. My mother doesn't know this. She assumes I go to the mosque near campus on Fridays, and I don't correct her. The truth is I stopped feeling anything during prayer around the time I turned forty. The motions are still in my body — I could do them in my sleep — but the connection isn't there. And yet sitting there today, hearing the adhan bounce off those old walls while I cracked pistachios, I felt something close to what prayer used to give me. Stillness. Presence. The taste of salt and the sound of something ancient happening around me.

I don't know what to do with that. I can't tell my mother I've replaced prayer with pistachios and a bench. That would break her heart. But I also can't pretend the mosque still does for me what it used to. I'm stuck between what's honest and what's kind.

Nudge: "You described that moment on the bench as close to what prayer used to give you. What do you think changed?"

Response: I think I stopped believing that anyone was listening. Not God necessarily — I haven't worked that out — but the structure of it. The imam, the rows, the routine. It started to feel like I was performing for an audience that included my dead father and my living mother but not me. On the bench I wasn't performing for anyone. I was just a man eating pistachios in the late sun, and that was enough. Maybe what I lost wasn't faith. Maybe I lost the ability to feel things inside a frame someone else built, and I can only feel them when I stumble into them on my own. That's a lonely thought. But it felt true sitting there.

My cohort went out for dinner last night at a rooftop restaurant in Jabal Amman. I almost didn't go. I had reading to finish and my mother had made lentil soup, and leaving her eating alone feels wrong even when she tells me to go. But I went, and I'm glad I did.

There were eight of us, all at different stages. Layla is twenty-seven and finishing her masters. Khaled is thirty-three and already published twice. And then there's me, the oldest by a decade and a half, ordering an extra plate of mezze because the lamb was extraordinary and the arak was cold and the evening air on that rooftop was exactly right. Layla asked me once why I came back to school so late, and I told her the truth — I wanted to, and I finally could, and life is too short to keep postponing the things that interest you.

She looked at me like I'd said something profound. I don't think it was. I think it was just unusual for someone my age in our context to say out loud that wanting something was reason enough to pursue it. Most of the men my brother's age would find that embarrassing. A man approaching fifty should have his affairs settled, not be starting over in a seminar room. But I'm not sure I care about what those men think anymore. The lamb was perfect. The conversation was good. I walked home through the old streets and felt awake in a way that the library doesn't give me.

Nudge: "You said you're not sure you care anymore what men your brother's age think. When did that start to shift?"

Spent the morning transcribing interviews for my fieldwork chapter. One of my subjects, a woman in her seventies from Irbid, described how she prepares mansaf for her family exactly the way her mother taught her, down to the specific way she folds the bread under the rice. She said she doesn't think about why she does it that way. She just does. Her hands know.

That's the thing I'm trying to capture in this dissertation and I keep circling around it without landing. The knowledge that lives in practice, not in explanation. My advisor wants me to theorize it and I understand why, but there's something that gets lost when you turn a grandmother's hands into a framework. I don't have a solution yet. I just know the gap is there and it bothers me.

My mother made knafeh today. She does this maybe twice a year, always unannounced, and when I came home from campus the whole apartment smelled like orange blossom and melted cheese. She was standing at the stove watching it with that focused expression she gets, and I sat at the kitchen table pretending to read but really just watching her.

She's seventy-six. Her hands shake a little now but she still moves through the kitchen with this sureness that I don't have anywhere in my life. She poured the syrup in one smooth motion, tilted the pan, pressed the edges with a spoon. Every step inherited from her mother, who learned from her mother before that.

I ate two pieces standing at the counter before she'd even plated it properly and she swatted my hand and called me greedy. I am greedy. I wanted a third piece and I took it. The sweetness was so intense it almost hurt, that good kind of too-much where your whole mouth is alive. My mother watched me eat and looked satisfied in a way that goes deeper than food. She was feeding her son. That's a complete sentence in her world.

Later I sat with my laptop open and thought about how this is exactly what my dissertation is about, but I can't write about my own mother. It would feel like a betrayal. Like turning something sacred into data. But she's the reason I'm writing about any of this in the first place.

Nudge: "You called it a betrayal to write about your mother. What would it mean if you did?"

Response: It would mean she becomes a subject. A case study. Her knafeh becomes "an example of embodied intergenerational knowledge transfer" and the orange blossom becomes a footnote. I'd be taking something that belongs to our kitchen and putting it in a seminar room for people who have never tasted it to analyze. My advisor would call that rigorous. I'd call it taking something warm and making it cold. I know that's not a rational objection. I just feel it in my chest when I think about writing her name in an academic paper. She deserves better than being theorized.

Canceled my library session today and drove to the Dead Sea. Told myself it was for thinking but really I just wanted to float. Paid for one of the resort day passes, the expensive one with the private beach and the buffet lunch. My fellowship stipend doesn't really cover that kind of thing but I did it anyway. Lay in the water for an hour, completely weightless, staring at the sky.

The buffet was ridiculous. Grilled prawns, tabbouleh, four kinds of baklava. I ate slowly and deliberately and didn't check my phone once. Drove home in the early evening with the salt drying on my skin and this lightness I haven't felt in weeks. I know I should have been writing. The chapter is overdue. But I can't bring myself to feel guilty about it.

Nudge: "You said you can't bring yourself to feel guilty. Is that new?"

Response: Not really. I've always been someone who needs a good meal and a change of scenery to function. The Dead Sea was beautiful and now I'm recharged. I'll write tomorrow. It's fine.

Sat down to write after the Dead Sea trip. Got four hundred words down, all of them bad. My mother asked if I'd been to campus today and I said yes. First time I've lied to her about something this small. Too tired to think about what that means.

Got an email from my advisor asking me to justify why my fieldwork methodology is "sufficiently distanced" from my own background. She phrased it politely but the implication is clear — studying my own culture's domestic practices introduces bias. As if the Swedish anthropologists who study Swedish folk customs don't exist. As if proximity is only a problem when you're from the region being studied.

I wrote a professional reply explaining my positionality framework and reflexive methodology. It was measured and appropriate. But sitting here now, I want to throw something. I left a career. I'm spending my savings. My mother eats dinner alone three nights a week because I'm at the library. And someone who has never set foot in a Jordanian kitchen is telling me I'm too close to the food. I need her approval to move forward with this chapter. But some nights the cost of fitting myself into their frame feels like it's eating the thing that brought me here.

Nudge: "You mentioned the cost of fitting yourself into their frame. What's the thing you're afraid of losing?"

Response: The feeling. That's what I'm afraid of losing. The reason I walked into a classroom at forty-nine wasn't the degree or the title. It was because something about the way my mother cooks, the way that woman in Irbid folds bread, the way my father used to pour coffee for guests with both hands — those things mean something, and I wanted to understand why they move me the way they do. But the more I translate that into academic language, the further I get from the feeling that started it. I'm terrified that by the time I finish this dissertation, I'll have a perfect theoretical framework for something I can no longer feel. And then what was the point? I'll have turned something alive into something correct. My advisor would say that's the job. But I don't want that to be my job. I want to hold both — the rigor and the warmth. I just don't know if the institution lets you do that.

Got back from the workshop an hour ago and I'm still thinking about the order we took this morning. A couple from Ghent wants a set of tiles for their renovated kitchen -- they want something "modern but with a nod to Flemish blue." I know exactly what Opa would do with it. I know exactly what I'd do with it too, and they're not the same thing.

I spent the afternoon sketching options. The ones that feel most like me don't really look like what the shop is known for. The ones that look like the shop feel like I'm just tracing someone else's hand. Maybe there's a middle ground but I haven't found it today.

Nudge: "What part of the shop's style feels most distant from your own?"

Response: It's less about the style and more about -- I don't know, I'll figure it out when I start painting.

Finished the Ghent tile set. Went with a design that layers the old Delft patterning under more fluid brushwork -- sort of like the heritage is showing through underneath. Opa looked at them for a long time and then said "that's not bad." From him that's practically a standing ovation.

Choir rehearsal Wednesday went well. We're preparing pieces for the Ascension service in late May. Meneer Peeters gave me a small solo line in the Lassus motet which felt good. I've been singing these pieces since I was fourteen and there's something about the way the harmonies move that I can't get from any other kind of music. It's not about performing, it's about being part of something that's been going for hundreds of years.

Also got an email from a gallery in Brussels that saw my portfolio online. They want to talk about a group show in September. I should be thrilled but I feel weirdly hesitant, like showing the abstract work publicly might make it harder to keep one foot in the workshop world.

Nudge: "What would change at the workshop if the gallery show happened?"

Response: Probably nothing concrete, it's more a feeling. Let me think about it.

Long day at the church restoration. The plaster on the south wall is in worse shape than the diocese estimated so the timeline is blown. Came home and Opa asked when the next batch of souvenir tiles would be ready and I just didn't have it in me to answer.

Writing again because I can't sleep. I know Opa wasn't trying to pressure me, he was just asking a normal question about the business. But it landed wrong. I'm stretched between the restoration job, the workshop orders, and now this Brussels thing, and I feel like everyone needs a piece of me that I haven't figured out yet.

My sister sent a photo of some mass-produced "Flemish style" coasters she saw at a tourist shop near the Markt. She thought it was funny. I didn't. That's what happens when the craft becomes just a product -- the hands behind it disappear. Maybe that's what bothers me about the gallery too, turning something personal into something on display for strangers to consume.

Nudge: "Tourist coasters and a Brussels gallery feel different from the outside. Do they feel different to you?"

Response: Yeah, I know they're different. The coasters erase the maker. The gallery at least credits you. I think what scares me is that once the work is out there, I can't control what it means to people.
Workshop all day, filled six orders. Hands ache. Opa showed me a photo of his father working at the same bench in 1952 and I couldn't tell you what I felt exactly but it was a lot.

Called the Brussels gallery back today. Told them I'm interested but need to see the space first before committing. The curator was friendly, said the theme is "inheritance and rupture" which is almost too on the nose for my situation. She asked if my work engages with craft heritage and I said yes before I'd really thought about whether that's how I want it framed.

After the call I went to Opa's room to tell him. He nodded and said something about how his uncle once turned down a commission from a department store because he didn't want to see the family name on something cheap. I think he was trying to tell me he understands being careful about where the work goes, in his own sideways way.

I keep waiting for someone to tell me it's fine to do both -- the workshop and the gallery, the old patterns and the new ones. Nobody's stopping me except maybe myself. But the art world wants you to pick a lane and the family just wants the shop to keep going, and I'm twenty-two and trying to hold all of it.

Nudge: "What would 'doing both' actually look like week to week?"

Response: I don't know yet. Maybe I'll figure it out after I see the gallery space.

Second entry today because something small happened. Was cleaning brushes after the evening session and put on the Lassus recording from our choir rehearsal. Started humming my solo line and Opa came in and sat down in the corner chair without saying anything. He just listened for a while.

When the piece ended he told me my grandmother used to sing that same motet at Sint-Salvatorskathedraal before I was born. I'd never heard that before. It felt like finding a thread I didn't know was there, connecting her to me through the same music, the same stone walls.

Visited the Brussels gallery space today. It's beautiful -- high ceilings, raw concrete, very contemporary. Standing in it I felt like my workshop pieces would look out of place and my abstract work would look like it's pretending to be something it's not.

Nudge: "What would your work be pretending to be in that space?"

Response: Pretending to be detached from where it comes from, maybe. I don't know, it's hard to put into words.

Church restoration is eating all my daylight hours. The diocese wants the south wall done before Ascension and the plaster situation means I'm basically re-doing the ground layer from scratch. My shoulders are wrecked and I keep getting home too late to work on my own pieces.

Had a thought while I was up on the scaffold today: the anonymous craftsmen who painted these murals five hundred years ago never worried about whether their work belonged in a gallery. They just made what the community needed. Part of me envies that clarity and part of me knows I'd suffocate in it. That's the whole problem, isn't it.

Opa asked if I've decided about the Brussels show. I said I'm leaning yes. He said "good, your grandmother would have liked that." Didn't expect that at all.

Nudge: "You sounded surprised by his reaction. What were you expecting him to say?"

Response: I think I was expecting him to say the shop needs me here. Maybe I even wanted him to, so I'd have an excuse not to go. Hearing him connect it to Oma instead -- that knocked something loose.

Ascension service rehearsal today. Sang the Lassus and it went well but afterwards Meneer Peeters said the choir might switch to a more contemporary repertoire next season because attendance is dropping. A few of us pushed back, said the old music is the reason some of us are still here. He looked tired and said he'd think about it.

I don't understand why everything has to be either/or. Why does updating mean throwing out what came before? The whole conversation put me in a bad mood and I skipped the coffee hour after, which I never do. Mama will probably hear about that and ask what's wrong.

Finished the south wall restoration yesterday, just under the wire before Ascension. Stood back and looked at it for a long time. The colours are brighter now but the composition is the same one some painter laid down centuries ago. I added nothing of my own and that felt right for this particular work. There's a kind of discipline in faithful restoration that I'm starting to respect more than I used to.

Told the Brussels curator I'll do the show. I'm going to bring pieces that sit between the two worlds -- the layered tile work like the Ghent set, where the old patterning bleeds through into something new. Not pure abstraction, not pure heritage, but the conversation between them. She seemed genuinely interested.

Opa helped me crate the Ghent samples this evening. He wrapped each tile in newspaper the way he's done it for fifty years, slow and precise. Didn't say much. Didn't need to. I think he knows this is me trying to carry the thing forward, not away.

Nudge: "A month ago the gallery felt threatening. What shifted?"

Response: Opa's blessing, mostly. And finishing the church wall. I'll think about it more later.

My mother-in-law rearranged Sami's entire nap schedule without asking me. She told Tariq it was better for the baby to sleep earlier so she could take him to the park in the afternoon. Nobody asked what I thought. I had finally gotten him into a rhythm that gave me two hours in the evening to work on a logo project. That's gone now.

I stood there in the kitchen holding a cup of tea and I just didn't say anything. Tariq looked at me like he expected me to agree. His mother was already moving the crib blanket to the other room. I could feel my jaw clenching but I smiled. I hate that I smiled. Something about the way everyone just decided felt like they forgot I'm his mother, not a guest in this arrangement.

Nudge: "You smiled but didn't want to. What stopped you?"

Sami had a fever all night. I was up every two hours. Tariq slept through it. His mother came in at 5am and took over, told me to rest. I should be grateful but it felt like being dismissed. I'm the one who knows what dose of medicine to give, what temperature means we go to the clinic. I didn't rest.

Nudge: "What would the right kind of help look like?"

Response: If she had asked. Just asked. "Can I sit with him so you can sleep?" instead of walking in and lifting him out of my arms. It's not the help. It's that nobody checks if I want it first.

Sami's fever broke. Normal day otherwise. Did laundry, made lentil soup, swept the hallway because nobody else notices the dust. Tariq came home and asked what's for dinner. I said soup. He said his mother already made something. So the soup is in the fridge now, I guess.

Finished a draft of the logo I've been working on. The client wants revisions but at least the base concept is solid. I had to work on it in the bathroom because Sami was asleep in the bedroom and the living room was occupied. Sat on the edge of the bathtub with my laptop balanced on the hamper. It's ridiculous but it's the only place I can close a door without anyone asking what I'm doing.

Can barely keep my eyes open. Sami is teething and it's been three nights of broken sleep. I had to email the client and ask for an extension on the logo revisions. Felt pathetic typing that out. My mother called to say my cousin Rania is getting engaged and when am I bringing Sami to visit. Everything requires something from me and I have nothing left to give right now.

Eid preparations are consuming the entire house. Tariq's mother has been cooking since dawn. She made ma'moul and knafeh and keeps sending me to the store for things she forgot. I don't mind the errands but she keeps correcting how I arrange the pastries on the tray. Told me that's not how they do it in their family. It's been two years, I still arrange things wrong apparently.

Tariq gave me money to buy a new outfit for Eid. I picked something I liked -- a dark green dress with embroidery on the sleeves. When I showed it to his mother she said it was pretty but maybe something more "appropriate for a young mother" would be better. I don't even know what that means. I put the dress in my closet anyway. I'll wear it.

My own mother sent a voice note about how she wishes I could spend Eid at their house this year. First Eid is always with the husband's family, she knows that, but she said it anyway. So now I feel guilty about being here and guilty about not being there. Sami won't remember any of this. I'm doing all of it for people who have opinions about trays and dresses.

Nudge: "You said you'll wear the dress anyway. What's riding on that?"

Response: It's just a dress. I liked it, I bought it, I'll wear it. Not everything has to be a statement.

Later. Wore the dress. Tariq said I looked nice. His mother didn't comment. My sister-in-law Huda actually asked where I got it. Sami grabbed the embroidery and pulled a thread loose, which honestly made me laugh. Small thing but I felt like myself for the first time in weeks. I held Sami on my hip and I felt steady.

Got the payment for the logo project. Not a lot -- 45 JD -- but I earned it. Put it in the envelope I keep in my underwear drawer. I have about 200 JD saved now. I don't know what I'm saving for exactly. Maybe a better laptop. Maybe just proof that I can do something that isn't cooking and wiping noses.

Tariq doesn't know about the envelope. I'm not hiding it because I think he'd be angry, more because he'd want to "help manage it" and then it wouldn't feel like mine anymore. My father always handled my mother's money. She'd get her teacher's salary and hand it over and he'd decide what the household needed. She never complained about it. I think about that a lot. I don't want to not complain about it. I want a reason not to need to.

Nudge: "What changes if Tariq finds that envelope?"

Response: He'd probably be fine with it. Supportive even. But then it becomes a shared thing, a family decision. Right now it's the only thing in this house that belongs to just me. I'm not ready to give that up. Maybe that's selfish. I don't care.

Took Sami to the park near the mosque this morning. He's getting better at walking on his own, barely needs my hand now. He kept crouching down to look at ants. We stayed for almost two hours. I didn't bring my phone on purpose.

Ran into Um Fadi from the building. She asked if I was working these days, meaning a real job. I said no, just home with Sami. She nodded the way people nod when they think they're being polite but their face says everything. I used to let that bother me. Today I just picked Sami up and pointed at a bird and we moved on.

Tariq's mother made mansaf for dinner and I helped her roll the jameed. She showed me her mother's technique with the wooden spoon and for once it wasn't corrective, it was like she was sharing something. I actually paid attention. Her hands move fast for someone her age. The rice came out perfectly and she said "you're learning" and I think she meant it warmly. These moments confuse me because they make me forget why I'm frustrated. Or maybe they're exactly why I stay patient through the rest of it.

Friday. Tariq took the day off and we went to his parents' village with Sami to visit his grandfather. The old house has a courtyard with a lemon tree that Tariq's grandmother planted. His grandfather told Sami the same story he tells everyone about building the house stone by stone. Sami just chewed on a lemon leaf.

I sat with the women while the men did their thing. Tariq's aunt made sage tea the old way, on a brass tray over coals. It was good. Quiet. I sketched some patterns on a napkin -- the tile work in the courtyard is beautiful, these geometric interlocking shapes I've been wanting to use in a design project. Nobody noticed me drawing, which was the whole point.

New client reached out through Instagram. Wants a logo for a women's fitness studio in West Amman. Paying 80 JD. I said yes before thinking about when I'd actually do the work. Sami's not sleeping well again and the house is never empty. But 80 JD is 80 JD. I'll figure out the hours somehow. I always do.

Nudge: "You keep saying you'll figure it out. Figure out what exactly?"

Response: How to be two people at once. The one who takes care of Sami and the house and keeps everyone comfortable. And the one who wants to make things and get paid for it and have something that's mine. There's no schedule that fits both. I just keep squeezing one into the gaps of the other.

Tariq and I had an actual conversation last night. Not a fight. He asked why I've been staying up so late and I almost lied but I was too tired to come up with something so I told him about the freelance work. All of it. The clients, the envelope, the bathroom laptop sessions.

He was quiet for a long time. Then he said he wished I'd told him sooner. Not because he wanted to control it but because he felt stupid not knowing. That stung. I told him it wasn't about trust, it was about needing something that was just mine. He said he understood that but it still felt like I didn't trust him. I don't know if he's wrong.

We didn't resolve anything exactly. He said he'd talk to his mother about giving me more space during the day so I don't have to hide. I told him I don't want him to fight that battle for me. He looked confused. I think he genuinely doesn't understand why I'd rather struggle alone than have him fix it. Honestly, I'm not totally sure I understand it either. But the thought of his mother knowing about the work and having opinions about it makes my chest tight. Some things I need to handle my own way, even if my way is harder.

Nudge: "He offered help and you said no. What are you protecting?"

Response: Can we not do this right now. I already spent all night turning this over in my head. I know it doesn't make total sense. I just need to sit with it for a bit.

Same day, later. Tariq's mother asked me at dinner if everything was okay between us. She must have heard us talking last night. I said everything was fine. Tariq backed me up. She didn't push it. But the way she looked at me -- not unkind, just knowing -- made me feel exposed. I keep building walls in a house made of thin ones.

The meeting with Diego didn't go how I planned. He sat there scrolling through my routing proposal on his laptop, barely looking up, and when he finally said something it was about the formatting. The formatting. Three months of pulling delivery data, testing alternate corridors, calling warehouse contacts in Medellin -- and he wants to talk about fonts.

I should have pushed back harder. Instead I just nodded and said I'd revise it. The whole walk back to my desk I was already rewriting my argument in my head, all the things I should have said. The numbers speak for themselves. Our current route through Bucaramanga adds two days and the clients in the southern corridor keep complaining.

Part of me wonders if I backed down because he's been here fifteen years and I've been here eleven months. That shouldn't matter when the data is right there. But I sat quiet like it did matter, and now I'm stuck doing cosmetic edits on something that should already be approved.

Nudge: "Why do you think you went quiet?"

Still at the office. Everyone else left at six but I'm going through the client satisfaction numbers from Q4 because nobody else is going to do it. Found three accounts where response times slipped below target because the team was running the old dispatch sequence I already flagged two months ago. Two months.

My mom called while I was pulling the spreadsheets. I let it go to voicemail. She'll ask why I'm still at work, then she'll ask when I'm coming home for New Year's, and I don't have answers to either one right now.

Nudge: "Are you going home for New Year's?"

Response: Probably not. There's too much to clean up here before January. She'll understand. She usually does.

Spent New Year's Eve at Lucia's apartment with a few people from the office. Nothing big -- arepas, aguardiente, the countdown on the TV. I didn't check my phone once the whole night, which felt strange at first but I stopped noticing after a while. Lucia made fun of me for not knowing any of the songs on the playlist.

On January first I went for a run along the ciclovia and came back with an idea for restructuring the southern corridor drops. Not the full routing plan Diego shut down -- something smaller, a pilot with just the Cali deliveries. If I can show a cost reduction on one leg, the rest of the argument builds itself. Nobody has to approve a pilot. I can just run it with the three drivers who already told me the current route doesn't make sense.

I drafted the pilot scope on my laptop sitting on Lucia's couch while everyone was watching a movie. Felt good to have something concrete again instead of waiting for permission. I want to own how this unfolds, start to finish.

The Cali pilot is running. Three drivers, rerouted through Palmira instead of the standard loop. I didn't tell Diego. I told the drivers it was a test week and tracked the first four deliveries myself from the dashboard. Average time dropped by six hours per run. Six hours. That's not a font issue.

I built a one-page summary with the numbers and sent it to the full operations chat, not just Diego. Maybe that was aggressive. Lucia said I should have gone through him first, and she's probably right in terms of politics. But if I go through him it sits in his inbox for two weeks and nothing changes. I've watched that happen before.

He hasn't responded yet. Part of me is nervous but mostly I feel like I did the thing I actually wanted to do instead of waiting around editing slide decks. If the numbers hold through Friday, the argument is over. I want this to stand on the work, not on who approved it.

Diego pulled me into his office yesterday and I thought he was going to tear into me about the pilot but he just asked for the full data set. No commentary, no lecture, just "send me the spreadsheet." I sent it within the hour. He forwarded it to the VP of ops in Mexico City. I know because the VP emailed me directly asking for a call next week.

That call could be anything. Could be a reprimand, could be an expansion. Either way the work is out there now and it's mine. I keep refreshing my email like that's going to make the calendar invite appear faster. Need to stop doing that.

Nudge: "What are you hoping she says?"

Response: I don't know. Something that makes the last three months feel like they counted.

Call with the VP happened. Forty-five minutes. She asked detailed questions about methodology, cost projections, scalability to other corridors. She knew what she was talking about, which made it feel real -- not like presenting to someone who just wants to check a box.

At the end she said she wants me to present to the regional leadership group in February. A full presentation, not a one-pager. My stomach dropped when she said it, not because I'm scared but because I've never had that kind of stage before and I want it to go perfectly. I already know I'm going to over-prepare.

Called my mom after to tell her. She said "mija, eat something, you sound wired." She's not wrong. I had an empanada for lunch and nothing since. But this is the thing I've been pushing for since I got here and it's actually happening. Not because someone handed it to me -- because I built it and put it out there myself.

Nudge: "What does a perfect presentation look like to you?"

Response: Numbers that nobody can argue with, questions I already have answers for, and walking out knowing they couldn't have gotten that analysis from anyone else on the team.

Stayed up until three working on the deck. Caught myself copying Diego's formatting from an old presentation because I thought it would make the leadership group take it more seriously. Closed the file and went to bed feeling off about it.

Lucia told me today that some people on the team think I'm "trying to skip levels." She said it like she was doing me a favor by telling me, and maybe she was, but it stung. I've been busting myself on this project and the fact that doing good work gets read as political maneuvering makes me want to throw my laptop into the Magdalena.

I asked her who said it and she wouldn't tell me. Fine. But now I'm sitting here wondering if I should tone it down for the February presentation or just go harder. Toning it down feels like giving up ground I earned. Going harder might prove their point. I don't know.

Finished the presentation draft this morning. Fourteen slides, every number sourced, three scenario projections. I ran through it out loud twice in my apartment and timed it at eighteen minutes, which leaves room for questions. My neighbor probably thinks I'm losing it.

Went to my abuela's for almuerzo after. She made sancocho and didn't ask about work at all, just told me about her friend Doris who's fighting with her landlord. Sat there eating soup and listening and realized I hadn't thought about the presentation for almost two hours. That's probably the longest I've gone without thinking about it in weeks.

Nudge: "How did the two hours feel?"

Response: Good. Like my brain got to stop running for a bit. I forgot what that was like.

Diego sent me "feedback" on the presentation draft. Half of it is adding his name to the second slide as a co-contributor. He didn't contribute. He sat on my original proposal for weeks and only engaged after I went around him. Now he wants credit on the version that works.

I know the smart move is probably to add his name and keep the peace. Everyone keeps telling me to "play the game." But I didn't build this by playing the game. I built it by ignoring the game and doing the actual work. If I put his name on it, what am I even presenting? Someone else's stamp on my project. I'm going to push back. Maybe that's stupid. I don't care.

Nudge: "Do you actually not care?"

Presentation went well. The VP asked me to lead a corridor optimization working group across three countries. Diego shook my hand after and didn't mention the co-credit thing. I slept eleven hours last night and I'm still tired, but it's the kind of tired where you know you did the thing.

Had an odd but decent afternoon. The laptop refurbishment group met at the community hall. We finished twelve machines this session, all Thinkpads from a school that was upgrading. Wiped them, installed Linux Mint, tested the batteries. Three families are picking them up on Saturday. One of the coordinators, Fatima, told me a teenager in one of the families wants to learn programming. I gave her my email to pass along.

Something about handing over a working machine to someone who actually needs it settles something in me. The work itself is mundane — thermal paste, partitioning drives — but the context changes it. I wrote cleaner code at work today too, oddly enough. Might just be the weather finally turning.

Nudge: "What exactly settles when you hand one over?"

Sunday, raining. That teenager Fatima mentioned emailed me, name's Ahmad, fifteen, wants to build websites. Sent him some links to freeCodeCamp. Felt good, but then spent an hour on an article about e-waste dumping in Ghana and now the laptop project feels like a plaster on a gunshot wound. Still worth doing though. Still worth doing.

Nudge: "You said it twice. Who are you convincing?"

Response: Myself, obviously. Next topic.

Ahmad replied with a screenshot of his first HTML page — white background, blue text, his name and "Welcome to my website" in English and Arabic. Simple as anything. I stared at it for a while. Reminded me of 1994, my first Geocities page about cycling routes around Lake Vänern. Thirty years apart, same thrill. That's something no article can flatten.

Sprint planning at work. We're building a route optimisation module for the fleet system. The PM framed it as "reducing fuel costs" which is true, but it also means fewer emissions per delivery. I didn't say that out loud because last time I brought up carbon impact in a meeting, Niklas from sales rolled his eyes. So I just nodded and wrote my tickets.

Nudge: "What would happen if you said it anyway?"

Response: Niklas would roll his eyes again and nothing would change.

Elsa called. She's been offered a promotion at the consulting firm, the one that does supply chain work for fast fashion brands. She's excited, better pay, a team to manage. I said congratulations and meant it. She's worked hard. But after we hung up I sat on the balcony and stared at the harbour cranes for a long time.

I keep thinking about what that job actually does at scale. The models her firm builds make it cheaper to move garments from factories in Bangladesh to warehouses in Rotterdam to shops that'll bin half of it in six months. She's good at it. The system is the problem, not her. I know that. But the thought doesn't leave me alone.

I'd never say any of this to her. She didn't ask for my editorial on the global textile supply chain. She asked if I was proud of her, and I am. I just also can't stop the other part of my brain from running. Maybe this is what getting older does — you see the wiring behind everything and you can't enjoy the lights anymore.

Nudge: "Could you be proud and uneasy at the same time?"

Response: That's exactly it. Both are true and neither cancels the other out. I just wish one would win.

Good day. Pushed the route optimisation feature to staging. Ran the numbers — if this holds in production, the fleet saves roughly 14% on redundant kilometres per week. I didn't put the carbon maths in the PR description but I did the calculation on a napkin at lunch. That's something. Not nothing.

Went to the midsummer gathering at Margareta's sister's place. Elsa was there, sunburn and all, laughing about her new team. I watched her from the garden table and felt that double thing again — the pride and the other thing underneath. But the strawberries were good and the light at ten pm was extraordinary and I let it be for once.

What got to me was later, driving home. Per, Margareta's brother-in-law, spent twenty minutes at the table complaining about the municipality's new recycling rules. Called them "bureaucratic nonsense." Everyone nodded along. I wanted to say that the Baltic is choking on microplastics and his inconvenience is not the point, but I ate another strawberry instead. I'm getting very good at eating strawberries instead of speaking.

Nudge: "What would speaking cost you there?"

Response: A scene. And Margareta's peace. And maybe Elsa's evening. So I eat the strawberry.

Still thinking about the drive home from midsummer. Per's voice in my head. "Bureaucratic nonsense." The man drives a diesel SUV and hasn't sorted his recycling properly in a decade, I've seen his bins. And everyone just nods. I keep replaying it. I should have said something. Not aggressive, just — something. A fact. A number. Anything.

But then what. I'd be the difficult one again. The one who can't let things go, who makes gatherings tense. Margareta used to say that. "You make things heavy, Henrik." She wasn't wrong. I do make things heavy. But the things ARE heavy. The sea is warming. The fish stocks are collapsing. Per's recycling bins are a rounding error but the attitude isn't. The attitude scales.

Third time opening this today. Can't sleep. The midsummer thing won't leave me but something else came up — Ahmad messaged me. His family's asylum case has a hearing next month. He said he's scared they'll have to move again. He asked if I could write a reference letter saying he's been learning programming. As if that would help an asylum case. But I said yes immediately.

Then I sat here and thought about what a reference letter from a Swedish software engineer actually does in that system. Probably nothing. The hearing will be about paperwork and policy and whether some case worker had coffee that morning. My letter is noise. But I'll write it anyway, and I'll make it precise, because that kid deserves someone putting words on paper for him even if the paper ends up in a pile.

This is the part that wrecks me. Not the big policy failures, not the carbon numbers, not the supply chains. It's the specific kid who's scared and whose future depends on a bureaucratic lottery. I can handle abstractions. I've been handling abstractions for thirty years in code. But Ahmad is fifteen and he built a webpage and he might have to leave. That's not abstract. That's just cruel.

Nudge: "You know it's noise. Why write it anyway?"

Response: Because doing nothing is louder noise.

Wrote the letter for Ahmad. Four drafts. Kept it factual — dates he started, what he's built, his consistency. Didn't editorialize. Sent it to Fatima to pass along. She thanked me three times, which made me uncomfortable. I didn't do anything extraordinary. I wrote a paragraph about a teenager who shows up and tries.

Work was long today. Deployment issues with the route module. Spent five hours debugging a timezone edge case that only triggers for drivers crossing from CET to EET on the Polish corridor. Brain is fried. But the Ahmad letter is done and sitting in the right inbox and that matters more to me than the deployment, if I'm honest. That's probably not what my employer wants to hear.

Nudge: "If that matters more, what keeps you at the desk?"

Response: The salary. And the route module does reduce emissions. Small justifications, but they hold.

Saw the news about the Mediterranean crossing. Another boat. I won't write the numbers because I've written them before and the numbers don't change anything, they just accumulate. I closed the tab and went for a walk along Eriksberg and the harbour was beautiful and that felt obscene, honestly. All this infrastructure, all this engineering, all this money poured into making Gothenburg's waterfront pleasant, and two thousand kilometres south people are drowning trying to reach exactly this kind of shore.

I know I can't carry that every moment. I know the guilt doesn't help anyone. I've read enough about compassion fatigue to recognise the pattern. But recognition doesn't make it stop. I still feel it in my chest when I walk past the kayak rental place and think about water as recreation versus water as a death sentence.

Ahmad hasn't messaged in three days. His hearing is next week. I keep checking my phone. The laptop group meets tomorrow and I'll go and I'll clean thermal paste off another batch of Thinkpads and it will matter to exactly one family and that will have to be enough. It's never enough but it has to be.

Coming back to this because the walk didn't clear my head. Sat on a bench near Röda Sten and watched the container ships come in. Each one carrying — what, ten thousand tonnes of goods? Some of it necessary, some of it destined for landfill within a year. I used to work on port logistics software early in my career. I helped make this machine more efficient. I'm still doing it, just one layer removed.

There's no clean position in any of this. I eat strawberries at midsummer that were probably flown in from Spain. I buy electronics. I fly to see Elsa when she lived in London. Every principled stand I take has a footnote of hypocrisy. I know the argument — individual guilt is a distraction from systemic failure — and I mostly believe it. But "mostly" leaves a gap, and in that gap I sit on a bench and watch the ships.

Ahmad messaged. Just a thumbs up emoji and "still here." I sent back a thumbs up. Two grown-ups and a teenager communicating in pictograms. His hearing is Tuesday. I'll be thinking about it all day at work, pretending to care about timezone edge cases. Maybe I'll bring it up at the laptop group tomorrow. Or maybe I'll just clean another Thinkpad. Sometimes the small thing is the only honest thing you can do.

Nudge: "Is the small thing enough, or just bearable?"

Response: Bearable. But bearable keeps me moving, and stopped doesn't help anyone.

Handed in my notice at Zain last week and everyone keeps asking why I'd leave a senior role with stock options. I don't have a clean answer. Something just goes dead inside me when I know exactly what the next six months will look like.

Nudge: "What does that deadness feel like when it starts?"

Response: It's hard to describe. Like the air gets thick. Anyway, the new place starts in two weeks so I'm focused on that now.

First week at NovaPay. The stack is Rust and Go, neither of which I've used professionally before, so I've been staying up past midnight going through documentation. The CTO is 31 and talks faster than anyone I've ever met. The whole office runs on this nervous energy that I find either exhilarating or exhausting depending on the hour.

My wife Dina said I looked happier at dinner last night than I have in months. She's probably right. My mother called from Amman and asked if this is really the time to be starting over, at my age, with the kids' school fees what they are. I told her it's a lateral move, which isn't exactly true — I took a pay cut. But she doesn't need to know that right now.

Second entry today because I just got out of the team standup and I need to note this. They gave me ownership of the payment reconciliation service on day four. At Zain it took me eight months to get write access to the production repo.

Spent the day pair-programming with Farah, one of the junior engineers. She's 26 and already knows Rust better than most people I've worked with. I kept catching myself wanting to impress her with something clever instead of just learning the idioms properly. Old habit. I measure myself against everyone in the room whether I want to or not.

We got the reconciliation pipeline passing all tests by end of day, which felt good. The CTO, Samir, pinged me in Slack to say "nice velocity for week one." I screenshot that. I know it's a small thing but I needed it. At Zain nobody noticed when you shipped. You'd push a feature that handled millions of transactions and the response was silence or a Jira ticket for the next thing.

After work I went to the gym for the first time in weeks. Ran five kilometers on the treadmill and my knees reminded me I'm not thirty anymore. Dina and I took the kids to City Walk for dinner. Layla wanted sushi, Adam wanted burgers, we compromised on a food court. Normal evening. But I was actually present for it instead of mentally rewriting code in my head, which is rare for me during a new project. Maybe that's a good sign.

Got invited to speak at a Go meetup next month about the migration patterns we're using at NovaPay. Said yes immediately. Now I have to actually build something worth presenting about, which is either motivating or terrifying, can't tell which.

Nudge: "What made you say yes before thinking it through?"

Response: Because saying no feels like admitting I'm not the kind of person who does these things. And I want to be that person. The one who ships and talks about it and gets known.

My old colleague Walid messaged me saying NovaPay's last CTO left after six months and the company has a "churn problem." He thinks I made a mistake. I think he's projecting because he's been at the same bank for fifteen years and can't imagine choosing otherwise.

The meetup talk is in three weeks and I haven't started the slides. I keep opening the deck and then getting pulled into something else — a new Kubernetes operator I want to try, a side project idea for a CLI tool that generates API contracts. My brain refuses to stay on one track. The talk would be good for visibility, I know this, and yet every time I sit down to work on it I feel this resistance like I'm doing homework.

Dina asked tonight if I've made any friends at the new company or if it's "all just code again." Fair question. I eat lunch at my desk most days. There's a group that goes to the shawarma place downstairs but I usually skip it because I'm deep in something. I told her I'll try to go next week. She gave me that look that says she's heard this before.

Performance review cycle started this week and Samir asked everyone to write self-assessments. I spent two hours on mine, rewriting it three times. The first draft was too modest, the second was too aggressive, the third I think landed somewhere honest. I listed the reconciliation service, the test coverage improvements, the documentation I rewrote. Five weeks of work and I already have a page of deliverables. That's the thing about joining somewhere new — everything you do is visible.

Had an odd moment during Friday prayers at the mosque. The imam was talking about the difference between ambition that serves your community and ambition that isolates you. I'm not especially religious but it struck a nerve. My father was a civil engineer in Amman who built the same kinds of bridges for thirty years and was beloved by everyone in the neighborhood. I can't imagine staying anywhere for thirty years. Does that make me restless or just different? He seemed content. I don't know what contentment feels like, honestly. The closest I get is the first few months at a new job when everything is a puzzle and I'm learning at full speed.

Dina read part of my self-assessment over my shoulder and said "you really need them to see you, don't you." She wasn't being mean about it. But it sat with me because she's right. I do need that. I've always needed that and I've never really asked myself why.

Gave the Go meetup talk last night. About sixty people showed up. The demo worked — the live migration ran without crashing, which honestly I was sweating about until the last minute. Got solid questions afterward and two people asked for my LinkedIn. Samir was in the audience and mentioned it in the team channel this morning.

Good day. Drove home with the windows down even though it was barely 18 degrees out. Put on Fairuz, which I only listen to when I'm in a specific mood. Dina had kept dinner warm. I told the kids about the talk and Layla said "baba, are you famous now?" which made me laugh more than it should have.

Nudge: "What mattered more — the talk going well or people noticing?"

Three months into NovaPay. The initial rush is starting to flatten. Not gone, but the problems are becoming familiar. I know the codebase now, I know the deployment pipeline, I know where the bodies are buried. That feeling of every day being a discovery is fading into competence, which should be a good thing but it makes me uneasy.

I keep thinking about a conversation with my uncle Faris last Eid. He runs a carpentry shop in Amman, same shop for forty years. I asked him if he ever gets bored and he looked at me like I'd asked something absurd. He said the wood is different every time — different grain, different knots, different mood. Same tools, same hands, but never the same piece. I want to believe that about software but I'm not sure I do.

Had a one-on-one with Samir and he talked about making me tech lead for the new payments vertical. It's a big deal. More responsibility, more visibility, a path to VP if the company scales. On paper it's everything I've been working toward. But there's a part of me that immediately started thinking about what else is out there. A friend forwarded me a job posting for a robotics startup in Abu Dhabi doing computer vision for warehouse automation. Completely different domain. I bookmarked it and I don't know why. I have a good thing here. Why can't I just stay with it?

Nudge: "What would happen if you stayed this time?"

Dina found the Abu Dhabi job posting in my browser history. She didn't yell, just got very quiet, which is worse. I told her I was just looking, which is true. Everyone looks.

Nudge: "What was it like when she went quiet?"

Response: Like she was doing math in her head. Calculating how many times we've done this. The kids changed schools twice in four years because of me. I know she's tired of it. I'm tired of it too, honestly. But the looking feels like breathing — I don't know how to stop.

Second entry today because I can't sleep. It's 1am and I've been lying here thinking about the tech lead offer. Samir wants an answer by Monday. The role is everything that would look right on a CV — leadership, equity, a team of eight reporting to me. My father would have understood this kind of trajectory even if he didn't understand the technology. Build something, get recognized, build more.

But then I picture myself in six months running the same standups, managing the same sprint cycles, dealing with stakeholder calls about roadmap timelines. The operational weight of it. I know the excitement would drain out and I'd be left holding a title that looks impressive but feels like a cage. This has happened before. At Zain, at the logistics company before that, at the consulting firm before that. The pattern is so clear that I could draw it on my whiteboard — six months of fire, six months of friction, then I leave.

I don't know what I actually want. I thought I wanted to prove I could build things that matter and have people notice. And I can. I have. But the moment it becomes routine I start suffocating. And I don't know if the problem is the jobs or if the problem is me. Dina asked me once, years ago, whether I'm running toward something or away from something. I laughed it off. Tonight it doesn't feel funny.

Nudge: "Dina's question — what do you think now?"

Response: I think I'm doing both. Running toward the next thing because it makes me feel alive, and running from the part where being good at something stops being enough and you actually have to sit with yourself. My father could sit with himself. I never learned how. Maybe that's what I'm actually avoiding — not boredom, but stillness.

Spent most of the morning grinding ink and laying out rice paper for the lotus series. Got through three decent studies before Xiaoli called to say Ma was refusing to eat lunch again. Took the bus over and sat with her for an hour, spooning congee and telling her about the lotus paintings. She ate about half the bowl, which is better than yesterday.

Back at the studio by three. The light was already shifting but I managed to finish one piece I had been circling for weeks. Something about the afternoon angle gave the petals the translucency I wanted. Xiaoli texted to thank me for going over. She worries she cannot handle Ma alone during the weekday shifts at the restaurant, and honestly she is probably right. But today both things worked out: Ma ate, the painting came together.

Small day. But the kind where you go to bed feeling like you showed up where it counted.

Nudge: "What made today feel different from other days?"

Response: Different? I do not think it was so different. Maybe I just had enough energy for once. Anyway the lotus piece still needs another layer, so tomorrow is already planned.

My daughter Yuwen called from Kaohsiung tonight. She has been offered a position in Singapore and she wants to take it. Two-year contract, some biotech firm. She was buzzing, talking fast the way she does when she has already made up her mind but wants me to say it is okay.

I said congratulations. I asked about the salary and the housing situation and whether she had looked into the health insurance. Normal questions. She said I sounded flat and I told her I was just tired from painting all day, which was half true. The other half is that Singapore is far and my chest got tight when she said January start date.

I do not think I am being unreasonable by needing a moment. She is thirty-four, she does not need my permission. But she called because she wanted something from me and I could not quite find it in the moment. I will call her back tomorrow when I have sorted myself out. People act like you should just immediately overflow with enthusiasm but sometimes you need to sit with the thing first.

Nudge: "What were you hoping to say to her?"

Response: That I am proud. That she should go. That I will be fine. All of which is true but none of which came out because I was too busy bracing for how quiet it will be once she is actually gone. Xiaoli has the restaurant, Ma has her routines, and Yuwen visits every other weekend. Without that I am just a woman alone in a studio talking to paper.

Called Yuwen back this morning like I said I would. Told her Singapore sounds like a strong move and that I want to hear everything about the lab once she has her first week under her belt. She sounded relieved. I could hear it in the way her voice loosened up.

After we hung up I went to the studio and started a new piece, something looser than the lotus series. Just playing with ink density on wet paper, watching the way it bleeds outward. No plan, no composition sketch. It has been months since I painted without a destination in mind and it felt like stretching a muscle I had forgotten about.

Dropped off steamed buns at Xiaoli's restaurant around noon. The lunch crowd was thin so we actually sat and ate together for twenty minutes. She asked about Yuwen and I told her. She squeezed my arm and said she is not going anywhere. I know that. But it was good to hear.

Had the calligraphy kids over today. Eight of them now, the youngest is six and keeps holding the brush like a pencil no matter how many times I adjust her grip. I should be more patient about it but something about watching her try so hard makes me want to get it right for her. I sat behind her and guided her hand through the first three strokes of her name. She laughed when the ink splattered and I let her keep the ruined sheet.

Their parents pick them up and sometimes linger to chat. Mrs. Huang brought tangerines for everyone and asked if I would consider teaching an adult class. I said I would think about it, but what I was really thinking about was how full the studio felt with eight small people breathing and concentrating in it. That kind of fullness is hard to manufacture.

Nudge: "What does that fullness give you?"

Response: Messy tables and ink on the floor, mostly. But I suppose a reason to set up and show up that is not just about finishing another painting for no one in particular. Anyway, Mrs. Huang's tangerines were excellent. I should ask her where she gets them.

Gallery owner Mr. Tsai called again. Third time this year. He wants to include three of my pieces in a group show in Taichung next March. I would need to deliver the work in person and attend the opening weekend, which means two nights away.

I told him I would check my schedule and he said Meiling, you always say that. He is not wrong. I know the exposure would be good and Xiaoli keeps telling me I should put myself out there more. But Ma has her bad stretches and I cannot just hand that off. Xiaoli has the restaurant to run. I am not making excuses, I am describing the situation. Two days away means two days where nobody checks if Ma ate her congee.

Nudge: "What would it take for those two days to feel possible?"

Response: Probably just knowing someone would actually sit with Ma the way I do. Not just drop off food but sit there and talk to her and make sure she finishes. Xiaoli tries but she rushes. I do not blame her, the restaurant does not close itself. Maybe I could ask Mrs. Huang. Or maybe I am overcomplicating something that does not need to be this hard.

Ma fell this morning. Nothing broken, just a bruised hip. Xiaoli found her on the bathroom floor and called me in a panic. By the time I got there the paramedics had already checked her over and said to watch for swelling. We moved her to the couch and she complained the whole time that we were making a fuss.

Stayed with her until evening. Read her the newspaper, made her favorite sweet potato soup. She fell asleep around eight and I sat there watching her breathe for a while before Xiaoli came to take over for the night. I am tired in a way that has nothing to do with sleep. But she is okay. She is okay and that is the only thing that matters right now.

Xiaoli called after midnight crying, saying she cannot keep doing this. I told her we will figure it out together. I do not know what that looks like yet but I meant it.

Nudge: "What does 'together' look like for you two?"

Response: I do not know, I just said that. Probably me doing more of the morning shifts with Ma so Xiaoli can open the restaurant without worrying. We have always sorted things out between us, this will not be different.

Hired a part-time caregiver for Ma, three mornings a week. Her name is Ah-Fen, mid-forties, came recommended by Mrs. Huang's neighbor. Ma did not want a stranger in the house and told me so in Shanghainese so Ah-Fen would not understand. I told Ma to give it one week.

Ah-Fen brought her own apron and a thermos of ginger tea. She was gentle, did not talk too much, and knew how to help Ma to the bathroom without making her feel small. By the time I left, Ma was showing her the photo album from our old apartment in Shanghai. I drove to the studio and painted for four hours straight, the longest unbroken stretch in weeks. I am not sure if I feel relieved or guilty. Both, probably.

Nudge: "Where does the guilt come from?"

Response: From the same place it always comes from. Anyway, the important thing is Ma seemed comfortable with Ah-Fen by the end. If this works out, Xiaoli and I can both breathe a little.

Yuwen flew in from Kaohsiung to say goodbye before Singapore. She brought me a new set of Kolinsky brushes, the kind I would never buy myself. We sat in the studio and painted together for the first time since she was a teenager.

New Year's Eve. Xiaoli closed the restaurant early and we all ate together at Ma's place. Yuwen leaves on the third. I keep looking at her across the table and memorizing how she holds her chopsticks.

Nudge: "What are you holding onto?"

Response: The way things are right now, I think. Everyone at one table. Ma complaining about the fish being overcooked. Xiaoli laughing too loud. Yuwen sneaking rice to the neighbor's cat. I know this exact configuration will not happen again for a long time and I am trying to press it flat like a leaf in a book. I have spent my whole life making sure the people around me are okay and now one of them is leaving and I cannot follow. That is the deal. You raise them to go.

Woke up early, first day of the year. The studio was cold so I boiled water for tea and just stood there holding the cup until my hands warmed up. Started grinding ink out of habit more than intention. Then I began painting: a small sparrow on a bare branch. Not sure why. It just came.

Yuwen stopped by before noon to drop off a bag of groceries she bought for me. She organized my fridge, threw out three containers of expired things, and lectured me about eating properly when she is gone. She sounded exactly like my mother thirty years ago and I almost told her that but stopped myself because she would have rolled her eyes. I just said thank you and walked her to the scooter.

Mr. Tsai's show is in March. I told Ah-Fen she might need to do a full weekend. She said no problem. Xiaoli said she would check on Ma both evenings. Yuwen said she would video call Ma every Sunday from Singapore. Everyone just stepped in without me asking. I have been holding all the pieces so tightly and it turns out some of them can hold themselves. The sparrow painting is still wet on the table. Small thing, but I think it is good.

Nudge: "How does it feel to not be the one holding everything?"
Long day. Lost the Hendricks account because their new facilities guy wants to go with a national chain. Grandpa built that relationship in '89 and I just watched it walk out the door.

Dad brought up the Hendricks thing at Sunday dinner. In front of everyone. Said maybe I should have driven out there in person instead of sending a proposal over email. Like I haven't been running seventy hours a week already.

Nudge: "What stung most about your dad's comment?"

Response: That he might be right. Grandpa would've driven out there.

Closed the Meyer Medical Group contract today — biggest commercial deal we've landed this quarter. Stayed late to call Grandpa and tell him before the rest of the family heard. He said "that's my boy" and I don't think I've smiled like that in a month.

Had a call with the regional HVAC association about their young leaders program. They want me to apply for the board. On paper it's a great opportunity — good network, visibility in the industry, looks sharp on a resume. But the meetings are every other Thursday evening, which is when I do my site walkthroughs with the install crews.

Those walkthroughs aren't required. Nobody would notice if I stopped. But Grandpa drilled it into me when I started: you show up where the work happens, you shake the hands of the guys turning the wrenches. It's how you earn trust. Feels wrong to swap that out for a conference call with people trading business cards. But then I look at some of the other managers my age who are on three boards and speaking at conferences while I'm in a crawlspace checking ductwork.

Nudge: "Which one would keep you up at night?"

Response: The walkthroughs. No question. I'd never forgive myself otherwise.

Thinking more about the board thing from yesterday. Turned it down this morning. Polite email, said maybe next year. But now I'm sitting with this feeling like I just closed a door I shouldn't have. My buddy Jake from college is VP of Operations at a company half our size. He's thirty-two. He's on two industry boards, he did that leadership program at Northwestern, and every time I see his LinkedIn I feel this pull in my chest like I'm falling behind some invisible race.

I know comparison is poison. I know that. But it's hard when the scoreboard is right there on your phone. Jake doesn't have a grandfather who spent fifty years building something with his name on it. Jake doesn't sit at a dinner table every Sunday hearing about how things were done the right way. Jake just has a title and a trajectory and nobody asking him to preserve anything.

Meanwhile I'm trying to grow this company AND keep it feeling like the company it's always been. Like those two things are the same direction. Most days they are. But days like today they feel like opposite ends of a rope and I'm standing in the middle.

Nudge: "Which parts of 'the right way' do you actually believe?"

Just realized I wrote twice in one day. Whatever. Came home and Jake posted about keynoting some supply chain conference. Good for him. I'm going to go prep my Q2 numbers because at least those are mine.

Nudge: "What do your Q2 numbers prove that Jake's keynote doesn't?"

Response: That I actually built something real. Forty-three paychecks.

Grandpa's 80th birthday party Saturday. The whole family came — uncles, cousins, people I haven't seen since I was a kid. Mom put together a slideshow with photos from when he first started the company. There's one of him standing in front of his first van, WHITFIELD HEATING hand-painted on the side. He was twenty-six in that picture. Three years younger than I am now.

Uncle Bill gave a toast about how the company was built on handshakes and showing up. Everyone raised their glasses. I watched Grandpa's face and he was trying not to cry. He looked at me across the table at one point and nodded, this small nod like he was passing something forward. I can't explain why that hit so hard.

Later that night I went out to the driveway to get some air and my cousin Travis followed me out. He works in tech in Seattle, does well for himself. He asked me if I ever thought about doing something else, something that was just mine. I told him the company IS mine. He kind of shrugged and said "you know what I mean." I do know what he means. But I also think he doesn't understand what it feels like to be part of something that started before you were born and will keep going after you're gone. There's weight to that. Some days it's an anchor. Other days it's the only thing keeping me steady.

Performance reviews this week. Had to sit down with each of my crew leads and go through their numbers. Most of the conversations went fine but Danny — he's been with us nineteen years, started under Grandpa — pushed back when I talked about the new scheduling software. Said it was "a solution looking for a problem" and that the whiteboard system works fine.

I wanted to snap at him. The whiteboard system loses us hours every week in double-bookings and missed follow-ups. I have the data. But instead I heard myself saying "let's give it a trial run and see how it goes, no pressure." Which is exactly what Grandpa would have done — never force it, bring people along. But part of me thinks Grandpa could afford to be patient because he had decades. I don't feel like I have decades. The market is moving and if we don't modernize the ops side we'll be the company that did great work in the '90s and couldn't keep up.

Nudge: "What would it cost you to overrule Danny and just implement it?"

Response: His respect. And every guy on the floor who remembers Grandpa.

Danny used the new scheduling software today. Without being asked. Came in, saw the tablet on the wall, and just started putting jobs in. Didn't say a word about it. I didn't either.

Nudge: "You didn't say a word — what were you holding back?"

Response: Relief. Like I finally got permission to move forward.

Sunday dinner. Grandpa wasn't feeling well so it was just Mom, Dad, and me. Without Grandpa at the table the whole energy was different. Dad talked about maybe expanding into the residential solar market. I've been thinking about it too — the margins are there, the demand is growing, it's a natural fit. But when I brought it up to Grandpa last month he said "we're heating and cooling people, that's what we do."

I didn't push it. How do you push it with an eighty-year-old man who built the thing from nothing? He earned the right to say no. But tonight Dad said something that's been rattling around in my head since — he said "your grandfather doesn't run the company anymore, Ryan. You do." And I know that's technically true. My name is on the decisions. But it doesn't feel true.
