It feels like I'm managing a museum and every choice I make is either a restoration or a renovation and I can never tell which one the family wants.

I keep going back to that nod at his birthday party. What was he passing to me exactly? The company as it is? Or the company as whatever I can make it? I genuinely don't know. And I'm scared to ask because what if the answer is something I can't live with.

Nudge: "What would change if he said 'make it yours'?"

Fourth of July at the lake house. The whole family thing — burgers, fireworks, Uncle Bill doing his annual speech about "what this country was built on." I usually tune it out but this year he looked at me during the part about "carrying the torch" and I realized he wasn't just talking about America.

Cousin Travis was there again. This time he asked how the company was doing and I gave him the numbers — up fourteen percent year over year, best Q2 since 2019, retention above ninety percent. He nodded and said "so it's doing well?" and I said yes. Then he asked "but are YOU doing well?" and I didn't have an answer. I said something about being busy, which is true but not what he was asking. The honest answer is I don't separate the two. The company does well, I'm doing well. The company struggles, I struggle. I don't know if that's dedication or if it's a problem.

Nudge: "If the company disappeared tomorrow, what would be left of you?"

Response: I honestly don't know. That's what scares me.

Had a weird moment today. Was driving back from a site visit and passed the old Whitfield Heating building — the original one on Division Avenue that Grandpa worked out of before we moved to the new place in '02. It's a laundromat now. The sign is gone, the awning's different, there's nothing left of what it was. Just a building someone else uses for something else. I pulled over and sat there for a minute. Not because I was sad exactly. More like I was looking at the future.

Someday the new building will be something else too. Someday the name on the trucks will change, or the trucks will be gone, or the whole industry will be different. Nothing I do is permanent. Nothing Grandpa did was permanent. The only thing that lasts is whether you did it right while you were doing it.

Something shifted sitting in that parking lot. Like maybe I've been so focused on holding everything together exactly as it is that I forgot the point was never the building or the sign. It was how you treated people, how you showed up, whether you left things better than you found them. Grandpa didn't preserve his father's business — he built his own. Maybe that's what the nod meant. Not "keep this the same." Just "do it right." I'm still not sure about solar. But I'm less afraid of the question now.

Nudge: "What changed between last month and this parking lot moment?"

Response: I stopped seeing it as either/or. It was never about furnaces.

The gallery wants to change the whole color palette for the installation, said it's "too somber for the spring collection." Spring collection. Like my work is a handbag.

Nudge: "What matters most to you about this piece?"

Response: It's about plastic waste in the North Sea. If I make it cheerful and decorative, I'm just adding to the noise. The discomfort is the point.

Spent the weekend at the refugee art workshop. We had twelve participants this time, up from eight last month. One woman, Fatima, has been coming consistently since October. She doesn't speak much Dutch yet, but her drawings are extraordinary - detailed botanical illustrations of plants from her home region in Syria that she does from memory.

I brought some of my old nature journals from when I was her age, thinking we might find common ground. We sat together for most of the afternoon, not really talking, just drawing side by side. At one point she reached over and corrected my shading technique on a leaf. I was the student.

The session ran two hours over. I had planned to work on my installation sketches this evening, but I sat in the car for twenty minutes before driving home, just thinking about those plants that no longer exist where she once lived. The commission deadline feels less urgent somehow, even though I know it shouldn't.

Nudge: "What shifted for you in that car?"

Response: I was thinking about permanence. My gallery piece will be dismantled in three months. Fatima's drawings preserve something real that's been destroyed. I started questioning whether my environmental commentary means anything compared to someone actually documenting loss.

Just got off a call with my sister. She asked when I'm going to "get serious" about retirement planning. I'm 52. I have a studio, commissions, work that matters. But because I don't have a pension and a holiday fund, I'm apparently not a real adult.

She means well. I know she worries. But every conversation becomes a referendum on my life choices. Her husband works in insurance, they have a nice house in Utrecht, the kids are in good schools. And I have paint under my fingernails and a second-hand car. Different doesn't mean wrong.

Later the same day. Maybe the call bothered me more than I admitted. I do think about money sometimes. Not having it, I mean.

Nudge: "What does security actually look like to you?"

Response: Not what my sister has. But maybe enough to not panic when a commission falls through. I've been pretending money doesn't matter because wanting it feels like a betrayal of why I do this.

Long week. The gallery accepted my color choices after I sent them a three-page explanation of the concept. They didn't love it, but they agreed. Small victory. I should feel satisfied but mostly I feel tired.

The nonprofit board meeting ran three hours last night. We're losing our main funder and no one can agree on next steps. Half the board wants to chase corporate sponsors, half thinks that compromises our mission. I sat there thinking about how much simpler it was when I just showed up and ran workshops. Now there's strategy and stakeholders and "capacity building."

Nudge: "What's getting squeezed out by all this?"
Saw the question about what's getting squeezed. I'm not avoiding it. I just don't have a neat answer. My studio time, obviously. Maybe that's fine for now.
Good day in the studio. Lost track of time, which hasn't happened in weeks. The installation is finally taking shape the way I imagined it.

Installation opens next week and I'm behind. Skipped the workshop on Saturday to finish the main panel, which was the right choice for the deadline but felt wrong in a different way. Fatima asked after me, apparently. One of the other volunteers said she seemed disappointed.

I've been telling myself that the artwork has meaning beyond just me, that it might change how someone sees the world. But sitting here at midnight with resin fumes and sore hands, I wonder if I'm just making excuses for choosing my career over the people I said I cared about.

Nudge: "Is this a one-time choice or a pattern?"

Response: Pattern. I know it's a pattern. The teaching job, my marriage, now this. I keep choosing work. I tell myself it's because the work matters, but maybe I just find people harder.
Opening night went well. Several pieces sold. A journalist from NRC wants to interview me about the environmental themes.

Same day, later. The opening was a success by every external measure and I spent the last hour sitting in my studio in the dark.

A collector asked me to explain the piece about North Sea plastic. I gave my usual explanation - the scope of the problem, the research, the materials I used. He nodded, bought the piece, and then asked if I could do something similar but "more hopeful" for his holiday home. I smiled and said I'd think about it.

I should have said no. I should have said art isn't interior decoration. But I need the money, and he wasn't wrong that people want hope. And maybe I'm the one who's wrong, clinging to this idea that discomfort creates change when actually people just look away.

I keep thinking about what I said in the last question - that I choose work because people are harder. But tonight I chose people, in a way. I performed the version of myself that makes sales and connections. And I hate it, but I also felt this relief that it was working, that maybe I don't have to be so uncompromising about everything.

Nudge: "What would uncompromising cost you right now?"

Response: Probably the commission. Maybe more. But also - and I hadn't thought this through until now - it might cost me this idea that my choices are purely principled. Some of my stubbornness is just fear of doing it wrong in a different way.

Went back to the workshop today. Fatima was there. She showed me the drawing she finished while I was away - a complete botanical study of a flower I don't recognize, maybe something that grew near Aleppo. We didn't talk about my absence.

I'm tired in a good way tonight. The kind of tired from doing something rather than just worrying about all the things I'm not doing. I emailed the collector to say I'd think about his commission but couldn't promise "hopeful" - I could only promise honest. He hasn't replied. That's probably fine.

Just got off a three-hour call with the fulfillment warehouse. They messed up the labels on two hundred units again. Second time this quarter. Spent the rest of the afternoon writing a detailed process document for them because apparently verbal instructions mean absolutely nothing to these people. My eyes hurt and I skipped lunch entirely.

Nothing dramatic happened today, just the slow grind of keeping everything from falling apart. Camilo was supposed to handle ops this week but he got pulled into a brand partnership meeting that ran long. So here I am, doing both. Normal Tuesday.

Nudge: "Does Camilo know you covered his ops stuff?"

Got into the Semana Emprendedora accelerator. Out of four hundred applicants, twenty-two got selected. I found out this morning and I just sat there staring at the email for a minute before telling anyone. Called my mom first. She cried. Then Camilo. Then Laura.

This is the first external proof that what we're doing is real. Not just two guys burning savings in a rented office. We got picked. Somebody looked at our numbers, our pitch, our traction, and said yes. That feels different from just believing in it yourself.

First week of the accelerator done. We presented our metrics in front of the other cohort companies and the mentors. Our customer retention was the second highest in the room. I know because I checked everyone's slides. Only that sustainable fashion startup from Lima had us beat, and they've been operating two years longer.

It matters to me that I know where we stand relative to the others. Some people in the cohort seem content to just be "learning from the experience." I don't understand that. You're here to win something—funding, connections, credibility. If you're not tracking how you compare, what's the point? The mentors kept saying it's "not a competition," but everything is a ranking if you pay attention.

After the session I stayed late talking with one of the mentors, Adriana. She built a fintech company that got acquired in 2021. She told me my numbers were solid but asked me what happens when I burn out. I told her I don't burn out, I recalibrate. She laughed. Not sure if she was impressed or thought I was naive.

Nudge: "What did Adriana's laugh mean to you?"

Response: Honestly, it doesn't matter what she meant. She's been through it and she's testing whether I've got the mindset. Mentor-founder dynamics are like that. You perform confidence. That's the job.
Sunday dashboard session with Camilo. Revenue up 14% month-over-month and CAC is actually down for the first time since launch. Finally something moving in the right direction on both lines at once.

Coming back to this because Laura and I got into it after the dashboard session. She wanted to go to brunch with her sister and I said I couldn't because I still needed to prep slides for Monday's accelerator check-in. She said I always say that. I told her the accelerator is twelve weeks and it's not forever. She said I said the same thing about the pitch competition, and before that it was the product launch.

She's not wrong that there's always something. But she's wrong that it's the same thing. Each one of these is a step. The pitch comp got us visibility. The launch got us revenue. The accelerator is getting us in front of investors. These aren't distractions from life—this is the thing. This is what I'm building.

I didn't say all that to her because it would've turned into a bigger fight. I just said I'd try to be done by two and we could go for a late lunch. She said fine. We went. It was fine. But I was checking my email under the table the whole time.

Accelerator mid-point review. Each company got fifteen minutes with the mentor panel. They liked our growth but pushed back on our unit economics. Said our margins are tight and that scaling with the current supplier setup could squeeze us. I knew this already. I've been running those models for weeks. But hearing it said out loud in a room with the other founders stung more than I expected.

The sustainable fashion startup from Lima—Daniela's company—got praised for their margin structure. She came up to me after and said something supportive, which was nice, but I just kept thinking about why her numbers look cleaner. She has a family connection to a textile manufacturer. That's not hustle, that's a head start. I know comparing like that isn't productive but I can't stop the math from running in my head.

Camilo told me afterward that the feedback was constructive and that we're still in a strong position. He's right on paper. But I don't want to be in a "strong position." I want to be the one they point to when they talk about this cohort in two years.

Nudge: "What would being the one they point to actually get you?"

Response: Credibility. The kind that opens doors before you even walk through them. When investors hear "oh, he was the standout from Semana Emprendedora 2025," that changes the conversation before I even pitch. It's leverage. First-mover advantage in reputation.

Skipped my cousin Mateo's wedding in Cartagena. Told my tia I had a work conflict. She didn't say anything but my mom called and I could tell she was disappointed.

Nudge: "Was it a conflict or a choice?"

Response: Both. The pitch competition was scheduled months before the wedding date was set. It's not like I picked one over the other—the timeline did.

We placed second at the pitch comp. Second. Out of sixty companies. The judges said our presentation was the most polished. Camilo and I just looked at each other after and didn't say anything for a solid minute. Then we went and got arepas.

Two investor meetings came out of the pitch comp. One is a seed fund out of Mexico City, the other is an angel based in Miami. Both want to see our full deck and projections by Friday. I've been working on it since 6 AM and it's almost midnight now. Camilo's handling the financial model while I'm doing the narrative and market slide.

This is what all the prep was for. Every late night, every Sunday dashboard session, every fight with Laura about brunch. This is the payoff runway. If we close even one of these, we go from bootstrapped to funded. We go from "interesting startup" to "company." The distinction matters. People take you seriously when there's money behind you. Suppliers negotiate differently. Hires return your calls.

Laura sent me dinner because she knew I wouldn't eat otherwise. She left a note that said "go get it." I almost cried, which is stupid, but it hit me at the right time. She gets it when it matters. I need to remember that next time I'm annoyed about brunch.

Mexico City fund passed. Said they loved the team but the market size wasn't big enough for their thesis. What does that even mean. Our TAM slide was solid.

Nudge: "What part of the rejection bothers you most?"

Response: That I can't argue with it. It's not about our performance or our product. It's about their model. I did everything right and it still wasn't enough because of something I can't control. That's the worst kind of no.

Miami angel came through. Signed the term sheet yesterday. Everyone keeps asking if I'm relieved and I keep saying I'm focused. Relief is for people who doubted whether it would happen.

Nudge: "Who are you performing that confidence for right now?"

Response: Maybe myself. The Mexico City rejection hit harder than I let on. For about three days I was running scenarios where both funds passed and we had to go back to bootstrapping for another year. I didn't tell Camilo or Laura. I just kept updating the spreadsheet and running the numbers. When the Miami call came in, the first thing I felt was my shoulders drop. So yeah. Maybe relief is exactly what it was.
Lit the incense this morning before the client call. Small thing, but my grandmother would approve. Felt steadier going into the pitch.

Met up with Jason and Wei Liang for drinks after work. They kept asking about my "startup life" with that tone they always have, like it's some extended gap year before I grow up and take over the family business like I'm supposed to.

I didn't argue this time. Just said business is fine, ordered another Tiger, changed the subject to Jason's wedding plans. I'm tired of defending myself. They don't understand that I can run my own thing and still be a good son. Those aren't opposites.

Sunday dinner at Ah Ma's was the usual. She made her braised pork belly the way she's made it for forty years, using the same clay pot that belonged to her mother. I watched her in the kitchen for a bit while everyone else was in the living room watching the news. She moves slower now but she still won't let anyone help with the cooking.

My cousin Melissa brought her new boyfriend, some finance guy from Hong Kong. Ah Ma was polite but I could tell she didn't like that he only spoke English and didn't touch the pig's ear dish. Little things. After dinner she pulled me aside and asked about my business. Not in the way my parents ask, like they're waiting for me to fail. She just wanted to know if I was eating enough and sleeping enough. Then she said her father also left his village to try something new, and that took courage, but he always sent money home. She wasn't lecturing. Just telling me.

I don't know why that hit different. Maybe because she's the one person who doesn't make me feel like I'm choosing between two lives.

Nudge: "What makes her different from the others?"

Response: She's lived through actual hard things. The war, losing her first shop, raising five kids. I think she just has perspective that my parents don't. They grew up comfortable because she made it possible. So now they're anxious about protecting what they have. She already knows you can lose everything and rebuild.

Still thinking about what Ah Ma said about her father. I looked up the village name after I got home. It's in Fujian, some small place I can barely find on the map. He left with nothing and built something in Singapore. Opened a provisions shop. That became my grandfather's import business. That became my father's company.

And I left that to start a tech consultancy. Three generations of building, and I walked away. At least that's how my father sees it. He's never said it directly but I can feel it every time we talk. Like I've broken some chain.

But Ah Ma's father left too. He left his family, his village, everything familiar. And that's why any of us are here. So why is my leaving different? Maybe because I'm not leaving out of necessity. I'm leaving because I want something else. Maybe that's what they can't forgive.

Nudge: "Is it something they can't forgive, or something you can't?"

Response: Both, probably. I don't know if I've actually forgiven myself for not wanting what they want for me. Every time the business does well, part of me wonders if I'm just proving I was right to leave. And every time it struggles, I wonder if I should have just stayed.

Closed the deal with the logistics company. Three-month engagement, decent budget. Should keep the lights on through Q2.

Called my mother to tell her. She said "that's good" and then asked if I'm coming back for Qing Ming next week. Didn't ask anything about the contract. I said yes, I'll be there.

Nudge: "How did that feel, her not asking about it?"

Qing Ming was fine. We went to the cemetery at 6am before it got too crowded. I helped my father carry the offerings, burned the paper money, did the three bows. Same as every year.

My uncle asked when I'm going to "stop playing" and help run the real business. Right there at my grandfather's grave. I just smiled and said business is good. Didn't want to cause a scene. But I kept thinking, would Gong Gong have been disappointed in me too? He built something from nothing. I'm building something from nothing. It's not the same thing, but it's not that different either. I wanted to say that. I didn't.

Nudge: "What stopped you from saying it?"

Fight with my father tonight. Real one, not the passive aggressive stuff. He came to my apartment unannounced, said he was "in the area" which is never true. He saw my laptop open with the client dashboard and started asking questions. Then he said he doesn't understand why I need to prove something when there's already a business that needs me.

I tried to explain that I'm not proving anything. I just want to build my own thing. He said his father didn't have the luxury of wanting. He had to build because there was no other choice. Then he said "you have a choice and you're choosing wrong."

I don't remember exactly what I said back but it was something about how maybe I'm not wrong, maybe he just can't see it because he never had to question what he was supposed to do. He left without finishing his tea.

My mother called an hour later to say he's just worried about me. I know. I know he's worried. But being worried doesn't make him right.

Nudge: "What happened after you hung up with your mom?"

Response: I sat on the couch for a while. Didn't do anything. Then I cleaned the apartment, which I do when I'm upset. Washed the teacup he didn't finish. Went to bed early but couldn't sleep. Kept replaying what I should have said differently.

Went to the temple with Ah Ma this morning. She goes on the first and fifteenth of the lunar month, always has. I don't usually join but I had time and she seemed pleased when I offered.

We lit joss sticks and she showed me how to hold them properly, three in both hands, bow three times. She does it with such certainty. I like that about her generation. They don't question these things. They just do them because their parents did them and their parents before that. I used to think that was mindless but now I think maybe there's something to just... participating. Being part of something older than yourself.

Had to turn down a potential client today. They wanted me to work on a project for their online gambling platform. Good money, probably six months of runway. But I kept thinking about what my parents would say if they found out. Not that they would, but still.

My grandfather's name is on my business registration. Lim Wei Keong Consulting Pte Ltd. I named it after him because I wanted to feel connected to something larger than just me and my laptop. So when I thought about taking money from a gambling company, it felt wrong. Like I was attaching his name to something he would have disapproved of.

I don't even know if he would have cared. He died when I was twelve. I barely remember his voice. But I remember my grandmother saying he never gambled, not even mahjong, because his own father lost everything that way. So I said no to the client. Probably stupid from a business standpoint. But it felt like the right thing.

Nudge: "What do you actually remember about him?"

Response: Mostly just impressions. The smell of his pipe tobacco. His hands, very rough, always moving. He used to let me stamp the shipping documents with the company chop when I visited the warehouse. Made me feel important. I remember his funeral more clearly than I remember him alive. Everyone crying. The paper house they burned for him. My father reading the eulogy in a voice I'd never heard before.

Mother's birthday dinner at the seafood restaurant in Geylang. The one we've been going to since I was a kid. Same lazy Susan, same chili crab, same argument about who's paying the bill.

Dad and I didn't talk much but it wasn't hostile. Just quiet. I gave her a jade bracelet, the kind she likes. She cried a little, said I didn't have to spend money on her. Then she asked if I'm eating properly. Same as always.

Nudge: "Where do things stand with your dad now?"

Response: Same as always. He's stubborn, I'm stubborn. We'll figure it out eventually. Or we won't. Family, right?

Client emergency all day. Their website went down during a promotion and I had to fix it remotely while pretending to work from a cafe because my home internet was out. By the time everything was stable it was 9pm and I'd missed Sunday dinner at Ah Ma's.

First time I've missed in maybe two years. I called to apologize and she said it's okay, she saved me some food. But I could hear something in her voice. Not anger, just... something. Like she was expecting this would happen eventually. Like she's been waiting for me to become the kind of person who misses family dinners for work.

I am that person now, I guess. The kind who chooses the client over the grandmother. My father probably missed a hundred family dinners building the company. My grandfather probably did too. So why does it feel different when I do it? Maybe because I was supposed to be different. I was supposed to be the generation that has it figured out. The one who doesn't have to sacrifice everything.

Nudge: "What did you think you were supposed to be?"

Picked up the leftover braised pork from Ah Ma's this afternoon. She'd kept it in the fridge all week, even though I told her she could give it to someone else. Stubborn woman. I sat with her for a while in the living room, watching her fold paper offerings for the upcoming Vesak Day.

She didn't mention me missing dinner. Just asked about business and whether I was sleeping. Then she told me a story about my grandfather I hadn't heard before. Apparently he used to sneak out of family dinners early to check on the warehouse. His own mother would scold him. "The goods won't walk away," she'd say. But he couldn't help it. She laughed telling me this. Said I reminded her of him. First time anyone's said that in a way that wasn't a complaint.

Nudge: "What did it mean to hear that from her?"

Response: I don't know. Good, I guess. Like maybe I'm not as lost as I thought. Or at least I'm lost in a way that runs in the family.

Got the async handler working today. Took me three days of reading documentation and watching my screen like it owed me money, but when those tests finally went green, I stood up and made myself a cup of tea. Just stood there in the kitchen, holding the warm mug, looking out at the grey Berlin sky.

Lukas, one of the junior devs, sent a thumbs-up emoji in Slack when he saw my commit. He has no idea what that little green checkmark cost me. But that is fine. I know.

Nudge: "What did it feel like, standing there with the tea?"

Response: Like I had earned five minutes of quiet. Not celebration, just stillness after fighting something.

Spent most of the day in meetings. Sprint planning, architecture review, a call with the client about edge cases nobody had thought about. By the time I closed my laptop it was already dark outside. Made some hummus from a jar and ate it with stale bread while scrolling through news I did not really read.

Fatima called but I let it ring. Texted her later that I was in a meeting. I was not. I just did not have anything to say.

Nudge: "What would you have said if you'd picked up?"

Response: Probably just the usual. Work is fine, eating okay, miss you. The same loop. Sometimes I do not want to perform that script.

The new hire, Emma, asked me to pair program today. She needed help understanding the ownership model in Rust. I spent two hours explaining borrow checker errors, drawing diagrams on my tablet, watching her face shift from confusion to clarity.

By the end, she said, "Tariq, you should do a lunch-and-learn for the whole team." I laughed and said maybe. But I meant it. That felt like the best two hours I have had in weeks.

Nudge: "Are you going to do it?"
Flying home tomorrow. Already packed my bag, already checked in online. I keep refreshing the weather app for Amman like it is going to change.

Back in Berlin. The flat smells stale. I opened all the windows even though it was cold. Fatima and I spent four days together and I still feel like I missed something.

Nudge: "What did you miss?"

Same day, later. Could not sleep. My brother called to ask when I am coming back for good. I said I do not know. He said our mother keeps asking about me. I told him I would call her soon. I probably will not.

Nudge: "What stops you from calling her?"

Response: Because she will ask questions I do not have answers to. When are you retiring? Why are you not here? I left at 56 and I cannot explain that to her in a way that does not sound like running away.

Did the lunch-and-learn. Fifteen people showed up, including the CTO. I walked them through memory safety concepts, showed some examples of common pitfalls, answered questions for twenty minutes after.

Afterwards, Marco from the backend team said, "You should have been teaching this whole time." I do not know if that was a compliment or a comment on my code contributions. Either way, I felt useful in a way I have not felt in months.

Got feedback in my one-on-one that I should "lean into documentation more." I think that means they want me to write rather than code. I nodded and said I would think about it.

Nudge: "How did that land?"

Response: Like being put on the shelf. Maybe I am reading too much into it, but it felt like being redirected.

Saturday. Went to Mauerpark, watched people sing karaoke in the amphitheater. A guy my age was doing a terrible version of a song I did not recognize. His friends were cheering like he was winning something.

Nudge: "What were you thinking while you watched him?"

Response: That he did not care if he was bad. He just wanted to be up there. I have not felt that kind of careless in a long time.

Same day, evening. Walked back along the canal, bought a kebab from a place near Eberswalder, ate it sitting on a bench. The light was that soft gold that Berlin gets sometimes before sunset.

I have been here six months. Half a year of takeaway containers and video calls and learning syntax. Half a year of proving I could start over. I do not know what comes next. The lease on my flat is up in August. The project I joined will probably ship by then, or it will not. Either way I will have to decide something.

The kebab was good. The guy remembered my order from last time. That felt like more continuity than anything else in my life right now.

Nudge: "What are your options?"

My manager asked if I had considered moving into a developer advocate role. Travel, conferences, representing the company to external developers. "You have the experience and the presence," she said.

I told her I would think about it. What I did not tell her is that it sounds like being put in front of people because they do not know where else to put me. I came here to build things. Not to talk about building things.

But maybe I am being stubborn. Fatima would say I am being stubborn. She would say any role is a role if you do it well. Maybe she is right. I do not know.

Nudge: "What would you lose if you took it?"

Had dinner with Lukas and Emma last night. They took me to some trendy place in Kreuzberg with exposed brick and cocktails that cost too much. We talked about the project, about their weekend plans, about nothing in particular.

At some point Emma asked how I ended up in Berlin. I told them the short version: got bored, saw a job posting, applied on a whim, got the offer, said yes. Lukas looked at me like I had told him I climbed Everest. "At 56?" he said. "My dad is 55 and he complains about learning new apps."

I did not know how to explain it to him. That staying in Amman felt like watching the same episode of a show I had already seen. That I needed to not know what was going to happen next. That the discomfort of being the oldest person in the room is better than the comfort of being exactly where everyone expected me to be.

I just shrugged and said, "Different people want different things."

Nudge: "What did you want to say instead?"

Response: That I was terrified when I got on that plane. That I still am, most days. But the fear feels more alive than the certainty I left behind.
